During holiday travel, it's fascinating to get yearly updates of how tech & culture spreads across the US. In rural Georgia:

· Uber works well
· 23andme is HUGE. No concern about DNA privacy
· DoorDash is everywhere
· Many people are doing side gig-economy jobs (like Rover)

1/4

· Media continues to decentralize. TVs that had Fox News last year had even more polarized, free Roku channels
· Even people who are not super technically literate are buying on Amazon. This is fairly new
· Not owning a computer, and using a smartphone, is common

2/4

· Old people are exposed to SO many scams. Calls, texts, emails, etc
· Climate change has strong doubters in rural America
· Not much tech backlash. Upon finding out I left Google to start something new, the most common view was that I am bad at career decisions

3/4

· There's a rise of D2C, just-in-time drop shipped products advertised on TV. COGs on Alibaba are $3, sold for $19 shipping from China
· The concept of pseudoscience email chain letters still exists, using articles shared on Facebook

4/4

@eriktorenberg People want two things: simplicity & low cost.

Simplicity comes from scale—better, integrated experiences. Amortize long-tail costs over all customers.

You can effectively cut *real* costs by selling less—focus on what people want, cut bloat.

These are at odds. Do one well.

If I ever work on another SaaS, I'll make cancellation easy.

When someone tries to cancel, make sure there wasn't a support issue. After that? Generate goodwill by not tricking people into staying as customers. https://t.co/7NlROvzDZM

Even further, most heretical views that exist don't come from "thinkers", and are often out of ignorance (ex: flat earthers).

Fear of appearing ignorant, along with the tide of consensus thinking, pushes most intelligent people towards consensus beliefs, entirely automatically. https://t.co/BbjosFfqh0

However, consensus thinking rarely innovates. Heretical thinkers—who happened to be right—have had a rough time.

If you want to come up with something novel, it's worth poking at and exploring. How? Start by reading from heretical thinkers who history proved right.

I'd offer that valuations are also higher because there was alpha in betting on good early stage teams. i.e., the market was *under-valuing* how effectively founders can turn nothing into something.

(This is unrelated to frothy valuations in late-stage companies. At a certain point, startups need to be businesses and justify their valuation.)

This explains why we've seen "person + idea" early stage startups—with the right person, and right idea—fetch high valuations.

$10m post could mean the business is "worth" $10m. Or, more likely, that there's a 20% chance it's worth $250m.

Since VCs can diversify, there's alpha in taking that bet. But this also brings in skill, decision making, and bias.

Who's benefiting from those big early valuations?

Not every capable founder can take the risks. Family, savings, and network all impact how easily they can go for it.

What does de-risking entrepreneurship look like for these people?

Not everyone has the network to scout, unfortunately.

Wrapped up a 3 day fast yesterday. Haven't done one this long in a couple years, and first time with a CGM.

· Feeling wiped out was the hardest part. Hunger was only bad during meal times.
· It was as if my blood glucose was draining until it bottomed out.

 📉 More graphs 👇 https://t.co/uGrF2wfImK

(Absolute levels are likely wrong. This was a new sensor, and they're not as accurate at lower levels.)

· Neat seeing super stable blood glucose.
· Hunger sensation was basically gone. It's such a cool way to improve dietary control afterwards. https://t.co/d8D1FuEAJ5

· re: feeling hungry, it's amazing how our subjective experience is so decoupled from biological needs. I could go from feeling really bad to great within an hour.
· Having a group doing it together for encouragement makes it SO much easier. cc @r00k https://t.co/TtLI1Pi69h

When I started re-feeding with a mixture of high fat / high protein / high fiber (lots of vegetables), it was as if I was slowly "re-filling", and my body was like "insulin? what's that?".

Then, it realized "oh yeah, I have a job to do." https://t.co/f7EcfEeTGu

@rcernst @LevelsHealth Using available hardware. There's a lot of options and many more entering the market soon. We're solving access to the hardware, too, so that it's easy to buy.

Going through @ewarren's policy proposals, spent 20 minutes with my wife going over Private Equity (incentives, goals, etc), LBOs, carry (+ tax loophole), etc.

Who's doing this for every major national policy position? Not politicized, clear explanations.

Here's what I want: 👇 https://t.co/9e0qNx3KSw

Every policy has winners and losers. Answer these things:

1. Here's how it is now.
2. Some people it's broken because X. Others think it's not broken because Y.
3. Here's the proposal. Here's who benefits, who doesn't, and externalities to be mindful of.

Then it becomes a lot easier to talk about UBI, healthcare changes, farm incentives, tax changes, student debt solutions, etc.

How can we make sense of things without understanding them? People are hungry for the tools to enable better sensemaking.
@inconvergent Would you do high quality prints of these?

First-order thinking breaks, when we go straight to solutions. But we need to understand how it is *now*, first.

Example: w.r.t. PE LBOs running companies into the ground it's hard to say if it's working as designed or not without understanding alternatives

Example:

We want people to have equal access to higher-ed, so solution: we subsidize student debt, fix lower underwriting standards by make not dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Yet, disconnecting market forces means costs still ↑ and we have a generation burdened with massive debt

re: Caltech v Broadcom/Apple:

Claim: Complexity has outpaced juries' ability to understand subtleties with deep tech. Even if they take their civic duty seriously, the right outcome may only happen by chance.

What does "jury of peers" look like in 21st century?

For criminal cases, selection of jury of peers has flaws (consider peremptory challenges, Flowers v. Mississippi), but is our attempt to hold citizens to standards set by other citizens.

It's an intentional feature of our adversarial legal system.

But for BigCo v BigCo, litigating Super Complex Topic With Significant Implications (ex: Google v Oracle), it breaks.

Idea: Assemble a group of

- citizens, protecting the common person
- domain experts, understanding the tech
- similar companies, advocating for market impact

For large civil cases, it's possible to align incentives such that experts and similar companies participate.

Jury selection could work similarly, with companies being selected (and then, possibly, challenged incl. for cause). Judges moderate selection as they do now.

Why this matters: there are prescriptions that on *average* indicate a higher risk, but do not directly mean that.

It's time for a Medical Data bill of rights, centered around access, privacy, and control. https://t.co/pI0bdlEjmn

I have a prescription for beta blockers (lowers blood pressure, scary!). Why? Qualified because "I've felt nervous at times giving speeches" 🙀

I wear a CGM (commonly used by diabetics) because I want to improve my metabolic fitness. My fasting glucose is 85  → I'm low risk.

Industry behavior like this disincentivizes anyone from exploring and taking ownership of their own health, because who knows what behaviors you'll do that put you in a "high risk" insurance bucket. It's the wrong direction.

Data brokers have been getting back to me.

Weirdest thing so far: LexisNexis has my email from when I was 12. I stopped using it then, and clearly couldn't have consented to anything.

So, they've successfully tracked me for 20 years.

And.. they give me info that's not mine 👇 https://t.co/RDcLcA5Joq

The data quality is dubious at best, which is worrying: they give me what they have, connecting me to auto insurance policies I've never been on (no clue who these people are) and cars I've never owned. https://t.co/j9BHWc0UdX

This includes the name, DOB, last 4 SSN, and last 3 driver's license number.

A quick search later, I found these people. Obviously no connection to me, but their data was included in my export because 🤷‍♂️.

Here's the thing: Data is a liability, not an asset.

It's fascinating to be able to test sense making and hold ourselves accountable to how we parsed reality.

One of our top jobs is to make sense of the world, yet most people don't spend too much time honing that skill. https://t.co/XaWO9JObyW

~60% (which *for sure* skewed low, due to who follows Tim) of respondents thought there'd be less than 2k cases in the US.

Since we can't possibly process all information, our lending of sense-making to others (friends, news organizations, Twitter feeds) is critical to hone.

I've been looking through tweets and headlines from 1-4 weeks ago. So many bad takes, clear biases peeking through.

Was sense-making poor or was what happened that unlikely?

Considering the people I see that had mental models that have performed well, I doubt it's the latter.

@tnorthcutt Examples: Trump's election, Brexit, the impending recession, covid-19, outcomes of ACA, etc. Happens in politics often.

Then do audits later. See who did well, and if possible, how they did well. There's people I respect a lot due to their ability to sense make well.

@tnorthcutt The fun bit is that it works for/against any political leaning, because there aren't incentives to be correct.

For many web sites, incentives are to get traffic. You may lose traffic if you're always wrong. So, a priori, I'd expect there to be a balancing effect.

What if: Testing athletes/politicians/celebrities effectively drew sufficient interest towards the problem to force policy, government, and corporate action?

Given a test capacity shortage—perhaps—the problem to solve is forcing hands through public interest. Make people care.

What I saw is a bunch of politicians and execs wanting to look good, and willing to put resources behind it.

How do we get that? Carrots and sticks.

Explainer on why oil is not *really* -$40:

Oil futures are priced for a time, location, and type of crude. The largest crude market (WTI) in the US is for a type called light sweet, and delivered in Cushing, Oklahoma. 1/n https://t.co/mBOiJUW0Ai https://t.co/ubghGEu8Y8

Literally, people are trading what they'd pay for a specific type of oil, at a specific time, at a place. You can imagine how changing any of these could change the price someone's willing to pay.

Ribeye steak in Manhattan now vs Flank steak in Topeka in 10 years. 2/n

Oil's price is down a lot because supply is higher than expected, and demand is massively down. There are ~few buyers, so if you have oil, you better have a place to put it, something to do with it, or someone to buy it from you. 3/n

Usually if the price is vastly different between places, you'd pipe oil from one to another. Hey, money. Or, if price now << price next month, you'd just buy now and store it.

But, storage is filling up. It's either filled, or already allocated (someone owns rights to it). 4/n

Even if in the future you can sell for $30, you've got nowhere to put it. Crude is basically toxic waste — you can't throw it in a ditch without a friendly visit from an environmental lawsuit.

https://t.co/GID6NAbGk7 5/n

On the day when they were settling the May oil futures, there was a mad scramble to *not own* it. Many of the people long were traders who thought they could store / sell it if needed, but they couldn't. So, they *needed* to sell. Price accordingly collapsed. To $10, $5, $0… 6/n

Then went negative. If you owned oil futures, you'd do anything to get rid of them.

All because of time and location. And as a disclaimer, it was at the very end of the market, so not *that* many people traded below $0.

Does that mean refiners were able to buy oil for -$40? 7/n

Maybe some who had transport capacity did (if that existed), but in general, nah. They had already purchased what they wanted.

Interestingly, the next morning, the June contract collapsed, because funds who were long June oil pulled out to avoid the same thing happening. 8/n

There isn't any oil trading at -$40 right now. June's is at $14.50. It was a market dislocation and panic for a specific time/type/location because people *had* to sell their oil futures and no one wanted to buy. It happened so quick, no one had time to do anything else. 9/n

If you want basically-toxic-waste crude in Cushing, the price is good (but not -$40 now at all). But your car won't run on that, and isn't in Cushing.

The price at the pump will go down, but it's like saying that spaghetti bolognese would be free if wheat was free. 10/n

$USO, an oil ETF, has a ton of exposure to the front month (the next contract in time), so it's been hurt a ton. It wasn't in the May contract during this, but may not survive much longer.

So, just because oil is cheap, it doesn't mean you can easily make money off it. 11/11

Reasons startups and smaller companies can move faster and compound advantages more effectively than big ones. Lean into these.

1. Ability to default to action vs "let's have a meeting and discuss"
2. Fewer consequences when (1) fails

👇

3. Less existing strategy you need to align with politically
4. Things that don't scale but are *incredibly* ROI (talking to every customer? sure why not)
5. Less time spent needing to do it "right" the first time

6. Going after markets that are "too small"
7. Ability to shift strategy as needed
8. Moving faster when opportunities present
9. Exploring new product concepts without disappointing big customers
10. Saying "we're sorry" and that being sufficient

This is a huge deal, but Bay Area vs Remote compensation likely won't converge as much as you think.

Though, may hurt Bay Area tech market a lot.

Here's why ↓ https://t.co/jwWBvO4mIC

In practice, at big tech companies, remote or colocated will become a team-based thing.

If a team is remote, then candidates compete with compensation. Nexus of talent may be in the Bay, but *for sure*, if they could make the same amount anywhere, plenty of people leave.

Geo-based compensation policies, then, make less sense.

Bay Area @ $300k vs Boulder @ $180k… it's hard to justify.

Wouldn't compensation gap converge? Given same qualifications and team is already remote, why pay ~significantly~ more?

Here's the problem: engineers at FAANG companies make ~1/4 in India vs HCOL USA. There are tons of teams there. So, how do companies excuse that if "location doesn't matter"?

FAANG will have tons of pressure to keep geo-based pay.

In practice, pushes a ton of roles remote to save money, and a lot of jobs leaving the Bay Area won't come back.

FAANG will still definitely pay top of market, but structurally needs to pretend that location matters.

Who else is bummed that The Office never had a chance to make a cryptocurrency episode?

Just imagine...

Ryan realizes that he missed the Bitcoin boom, so decides to create his own, Swagcoin. 1/10 https://t.co/uPU9o2brL1

Dwight loves the idea of leaving the dollar, but feels the coins should be asset backed. By beets.

Jim and Pam sees an opportunity to write a BeetCoin whitepaper, falsely dated to a few years prior. They share it with Dwight. He's destroyed—someone beat him to the idea. 2/10

Upon reading it closely, Dwight is convinced that BeetCoin, backed by "virtual" beets, is inferior to his idea, backed by physical beets.

He hires Erin, who has been learning coding in the evening, to build it. She agrees, but doesn't quite know what cryptocurrencies are. 3/10

Michael, meanwhile, is back dating Holly. She says she wants to get weed but her old dealer moved away. Michael says he knows a guy.

Michael asks his "urban" friend, Stanley, if he can help. Stanley tells him to shove it. 4/10

Ryan overhears, and tells him about the Dark Net, where you can buy anything. Ryan agrees to sell Michael 0.01 bitcoin for $1000: "This is a surefire investment, every dip is a buying opportunity."

Ryan gives him a physical wallet, with the "bitcoin securely inside". 5/10

Michael installs Tor, but can't figure out how to access his "wallet".

Meanwhile, Dwight has replaced the office heating system with crypto miners. Angela is concerned with the noise, and organizes a meeting to discuss. 6/10

Dwight, as the office building owner, realizes the electricity cost will be an issue. Creed offers to wire up the building straight to the grid, so electricity is free.

Michael is frustrated with the Dark Net and—with Darryl—wonders why the bad people's internet is "dark". 7/10

Pam confesses to the Office Workers Against Noise Committee (which is the same as the Party Planning Committee) that the original BeetCoin paper was fake.

Angela tells Dwight, and asks him to please stop mining. Dwight has empathy, and quietly shuts down mining operations. 8/10

Darryl says he knows someone who can sell weed for Michael's bitcoin. Michael, ignorant of drug prices, gives him the wallet.

The "bitcoin" in the wallet is just HTML Ryan printed out. Darryl threatens to expose Ryan. 9/10

Ryan installs a weed-delivery app, and orders for Michael. 

It's delivered, and Michael tips the driver half the weed.

Holly thanks Michael, but she discovered that weed is legal now and just ordered it herself. 10/10

@demonetizedblog I agree with you, but that example should nudge our bayesian prior at least a little bit.

The inverse is easier: identifying certain bad models. Any model that inadequately considers future uncertainty is a bad model. This can be inferred by the model's variance of predictions.

@demonetizedblog Example: if, for the 12 months prior to the election, the model oscillates between 90/10 and 10/90 every week, then we can be pretty sure it's a bad model: each successive "swing" means the prior estimates didn't take into account whatever caused the latest swing.

@demonetizedblog This is my critique of 538: predicting "if the election happened today, what are the odds?" isn't useful because no one can validate it, and predicting "what is the chance Biden wins in Nov?" is useful, but *necessarily* has high uncertainty, so the model variance should be low.

There's a common idea, re: free will, that even if there's no free will, it's better to act as if there is. Having that stance (you have volition in life! your conscious effort matter!) yields better outcomes.

I've found it incredibly useful to *also* believe the inverse.

Assuming: I'm an automaton, with behavior entirely dependent on context/inputs.

Thus, intentionally design the environment to accommodate "addict" behavior, because in the moment, I have ~no control.

I joke with friends that it's "supply side self control".

@tomgriff_in Of course. I don't think either *has* to be true to exploit the symmetry.

It's easier to make environmental changes than in-the-moment good decisions at times. So, even assuming no free will, spreading that awareness can be enough so that people design their environments.

@michelecanzi @benedictevans @profgalloway Evergreen tweet

The underlying message here rings true: it's worrying how  many assets are priced on perception of finding the next sucker, rather than—say—free cash flows.

However, imho, this view is incomplete, and this shift is an entirely predictable outcome of zero interest rate policy. 🧵 https://t.co/WAzL8mzg3q

*A ton* of investable assets have value that relies on future demand. This isn't new.

i.e., commodities aren't about funding innovation — every CTA strategy is trying to predict future demand.

There's also always been demand for houses, art, classic cars, designer brands, etc.

But, even in venture (literally giving resources to get stuff built), there's a need for liquidity eventually.

The market cap is *massively* impacted by perception of future demand — just look at recent IPO/SPAC multiples.

With cheap capital / ZIRP, valuing based on discounted cash flow breaks down. The present value is massively more dependent on *prospects* of future growth.

Who picks that price? Demand. $TSLA figured that out early.

The bigger change is how many people don't want traditional assets.

If everything's valued based on funny money, why invest in $SPY when they can invest in something new and exciting?

Add to that exuberance from a decade long bull market, cheap capital, and crypto FOMO.

What's the way out? Some of this is just lessons people will learn painfully.

On a larger scale, end our addiction to cheap debt and short-vol. This is hard, though.

Very large tech companies, internally, are sort of like centrally planned economies.

I’m curious how it works so well—efficiency wise. Amazon isn’t known for gross mismanagement of resources (obviously, exploitation of labor concerns aside, focusing on resource allocation).

Central planning and distorted tight market feedback loops breeds inefficiency.

Ex: governments, education, healthcare, aging large incumbent companies. Not known for innovation, or people working hard / taking risks / challenging status quo / thinking creatively about problems.

As systems centralize, they gain economies of scale, but distort incentive structures.

But in Amazon, internally, it’s basically centrally planned. Projects are funded based on company/org/team goals. Compensation is set centrally based on performance, but it’s mostly one-sided.

They famously lean heavily on equity compensation, but each person’s impact to equity price is ~0.

Is it that employment is opt-in, *and* they can fire people? Or that it’s not a closed system (engineer makes money at Amazon, spends elsewhere)? Or a management innovation?

Had potential… the virus… exposure, quarantining for a few days. Trying to actively minimize risk, doesn't surprise me at all that people turn to quackery: CDC guidance is common sense, but little active suggestions I should consider doing.

Minimize social exposure, got it.

But, what's the best research say about optimal virus-fighting diet? Supplements? Levels of activity and sleep?

It's like everyone went into CYA-mode, leaving kooks behind to dominate the narrative. Even if they told me what *isn't* shown to help, that helps.

I bet a lot of people would happily do some low-downside, somewhat inconvenient, not-fully-proven lifestyle changes to plausibly move the risk needle.

Like, we know metabolic disease correlates to COVID outcomes. No recommendation to cut out sugar? Entirely guessing.

Get extra sleep? Cut out alcohol? Minimize inflammation and oxidative stress with Omega 3s, curcumin, black seed oil, NAC?

I'm making stuff up here, but it's as if we're waiting on Science™ to officially bless things before any sort of laying out potential risks / benefits.

@TedDundon @Austen Kill step up basis. Assets can be held by estate/heirs on death, minus debt.

- you keep your farm, with old basis
- if debts are owed, they're paid with post-tax money, as they would be if paid before death

Solves most of the loopholes, and usurps "death tax" discussion.
@TedDundon @Austen Mind you, the reason mainstream progressives don't talk about removing the step up basis—even though this is the fundamental issue—is because it polls well in swing states.

@metajess @TedDundon @Austen Your heirs inherit your assets, and the point at which capital gains taxes are assessed from is not the price you paid (your “basis”), but the current value.

You could buy an asset for $100k, have it worth $1m on death, and the capital gains from $100k to $1m is never taxed.

@kyzcralotSxaM It depends on the market. Appraisers don’t take into account nearly everything that may become economically relevant.

When we bought ours, we discovered a leak in the sewage pipe, the hot water heater was EOL, etc. Seller fixed or credited the big stuff.
@kyzcralotSxaM In a very strong market, sellers sometimes just say “eh, it’s on you, or you can walk”. On weaker markets, sellers fix a ton of stuff to get a deal to happen.

@kyzcralotSxaM My parents, 10 years ago, were fixing tiny nail holes and paint scuffs that the buyer objected to, so the buyer would close.

Last year, when I bought, my agent told me to only pick a few big things and let everything else slide.

@kyzcralotSxaM Local agents can be helpful because they both know norms, and how hot things are. It’s all just negotiation, and the stronger side wins usually.

Our neighbors didn’t get nearly as many concessions — their seller thought worst case, if they relisted, they’d get more $.

It's interesting seeing divergences of best practices between theoreticians and practitioners.

⦿ Doctors / nutritionists vs athletes
⦿ Economists vs traders
⦿ Coding bootcamp teachers vs software engineers

It's not that practitioners are always right, but I've found that—within their incentive bounds—they approximate "correct" pretty often.

They frequently have a pretty good sense of "what would things look like if I was approaching this wrong?"

Was talking to an endurance athlete, asking about Zone 2 workouts to improve mitochondrial baseline function.

He was like "eh, after a certain point it's mostly mental, so I want to exercise at a level that gives high suffering to focus on psychological adaptation".
It also could be that practitioners default to action, which beats any theory (that never has to *do the thing*).

@samswoora It’s @nickgraynews’s cocktail party format. That’s the answer.

@samswoora @nickgraynews I tweaked his format (a wee less formal, and I like cooking so we do food), but yeah. My wife’s quite introverted, and enjoyed them.

Things we did, mostly borrowing from him:

@samswoora @nickgraynews - Intentionality around invite list. We wanted couples friends in our new town, so focused on that, making sure people would mesh.
- Found a few people who are *always* down. They get invited first, so additional people don’t feel like they may come to an empty group.

@samswoora @nickgraynews - Set clear expectations. Keeping it to a well-defined time is key. (“Dinner and light games, you’ll be out by 8pm” sort of thing)
- Keep it light and fun. Minimize downtime, have stuff to do and topics ready.
@samswoora @nickgraynews I think the overall trick is realizing that *hosting* is an easy way to meet people and have them excited to hang. Don’t need to wait until established groups invite you.
@samswoora @nickgraynews I also recommend The Art of Gathering. Really great tips, a lot of the same stuff, but more theory.

@samswoora @nickgraynews Totally. And everyone feels bad about “leaving early”. There’s no conclusion.

Let people know when it’ll be over, ahead of time. Then, at the time, have a send off. And if you’re having fun and want to keep a part of it going? Easy —

@samswoora @nickgraynews Dismiss everyone anyways, and leave the door open for *optionally* sticking around.

People who want to go will (and not feel bad), and others will stick around. Useful to, even then, commit to a later time you’ll kick everyone out.

@nickgraynews @samswoora I bet you’re tickled pink at the idea of thousands of little Nick-cocktail-parties happening over the next year, from your book.

@jonnym1ller @kellycwilde Instant quality of life improvement, highly endorsed.

⦿ Dog training is like human psychology. People disagree, a lot works. If something doesn't work, try something else.

@jonnym1ller @kellycwilde ⦿ Early on, entirely ignore the dog when you get home. Don't greet them—it reinforces excitement. Ours went from getting overly excited and jumping when we came home, to 0 of that.
@jonnym1ller @kellycwilde ⦿ Dog emotion is less about happy/sad/angry/etc, and more "high intensity" "low intensity". Managing intensity levels to be appropriate to the situation.

@jonnym1ller @kellycwilde ⦿ Dogs respond well to structure. Have a few rituals that establish structure. For example, dog must get approval before walking out a door; shakes before they can eat food; etc.
⦿ Don't ever let the dog in bed. It's your call, but if you forbid early on, they don't miss it.

@krishnanrohit Ohh. You may be thinking about integration or e2e tests. These are decently common, even on smaller code bases.

i.e., unit tests are very zoomed in. Sometimes it's useful to actually run a browser or something to make sure the *output* is what is expected.

@krishnanrohit rando link I googled that explains this approach: https://t.co/dZSPKjLS8l

(It's not a "super big codebase" problem, and more of "we have a bunch of components, and want to make sure they work together as we expect")

@krishnanrohit ex, if you had a mobile app, you might write an e2e test that actually runs the real app, opens it, and verifies the first screen renders as you'd expect.

Under the hood, it may be running tens of thousands of lines of code. You aren't checking all the parts, just the outcome.

@louis02x If it's really important, no one stops you from saving it. Over time, I stopped trying to hold on to them. I don't *think* I've missed much.

It's the same as with practicing anything: you're doing something intentional, might as well focus on that.

@louis02x There's also a lot of goals of meditation. If you're trying to learn to relax and let go, then holding on to good ideas might be counter productive. If you're trying to sharpen your focus, ideas distract you from that.

@louis02x If you're trying to realize the nature of thought itself (or how you aren't your thoughts), ideas present a great opportunity to realize their emptiness. If you're trying to cultivate specific feelings (like loving kindness), ideas distract you from that.

@louis02x Note that I'm not saying don't have ideas or try to suppress them — only that as they appear, you can either engage with them or not, and engaging with them may be antithetical to the reason you're meditating.

@louis02x Practically speaking, if it's important, it'll come back.

And maybe you'll lose a few. That's probably okay.

@louis02x Wanting to save an idea is two things:
- Having the idea, which arises automatically (you don't "think" the idea, it arises)
- Grabbing on to it, because fear of losing it (or similar)

It may be awesome, but giving in doesn't really help you deconstruct the grabbing reflex.

Something clicked for me recently, re: AI risks, wearable data, news statistics, etc.

Most people are simply not used to "facts" having error bars. This goes deeper than you'd imagine...

Your car's speedometer is roughly accurate. Maybe it's 2% off, but in general, what you see is what it is.

You step on a scale, and it's within a lb/kg usually.

You set your oven to 350°F, and it goes roughly there (and maybe you adjust over time if it's off).

You hear inflation was 7.6%, and 7.7% was expected. Markets go up. Nevermind it's a constructed metric.

And nevermind that "inflation", as a statistic, doesn't really apply to any specific person's spending habits to that degree of specificity.

Our brains interpret all these signals as "factual" or "not factual", when reality is far more messy.

I've seen this with glucose monitoring. The monitors give you a *precise* measure of glucose, and like a speedometer, it's natural to assume it's "true".

I've seen people do cartwheels explaining how a 5mg/dL change (quite small) was impacted by some variable they controlled.

Nevermind all the other dozens of variables that impact how fast glucose hits your blood stream or how much insulin gets released.

The same exists across a lot of wearables. Your Whoop recovery score is 10 points lower? Your Oura sleep score is 5 points higher? Your activity rings closed sooner?

I work in wearables. I think they'll be the future of personalized wellness. But...

These all feel like weighing yourself. And they're not.

AI has this problem. Models exist that give *answers*. Want a proof that all odd numbers are prime? Here you go.

And answers feel... factual. They don't imply error bars. https://t.co/rd5ZsWMqCP

They're designed explicitly to output something, and the better it sounds, the more we like it. Except, specificity (confidence) isn't what's useful — correctness is.

We were warned all along: current-gen LLMs are explicitly paperclip maximizers. They output what sounds good.

This confidence is dangerous, because many people haven't spent years thinking about error bars and loss curves.

Lying about a proof of primality of odds doesn't pattern match to things people lie about, so can sail past BS detectors.

People have lied with statistics forever, but I don't think we're culturally prepared to *everything* having error bars and uncertainty, especially if it doesn't match to what had uncertainty previously.

It's a good example about how technology makes something that, at its limit, yields a bad outcome, but steps along the way are reasonable.

Another example: AirTags. GPS trackers existed, but making tracking near free and available opens new threats, practically speaking. https://t.co/h7TI852IOL

As an AirTag user, it's annoying that they don't actually help a ton with finding stolen property, because they *notify the thief* about the tracking (including making it easy to find the device).

But if we blur our eyes, anonymous tracking enables stalking.

These are at odds.
We don't have the right cultural machinery to deal with these, where *practically speaking*, technology making something 100x easier/cheaper can yield some pretty bad outcomes.

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan Re: renting vs owning, having been through this also (made the spreadsheets, etc), I encourage you to not optimize for this. Or do it once to be directionally accurate, and then not worry about it.

It’s like deciding who to marry...

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan There are good and bad decisions, for sure, but the variance/unknowns vastly outweigh what you can consider. More importantly, the things you may consider might turn out not to be the primary important things.

I did this exact analysis, and bought a house. I love it. But...

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan · Assume, especially now, it's not a "good" asset. The house depreciates, and land value is historically super high now.
· If you want optionality to move in the next few years, don't buy. The only way you walk away ahead is if the value shoots up (see previous point).

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan · A lot of pro-buying advice you'll find is skewed by people who realized one of the largest national real estate booms in history.
· If you're financially stable, a difference of 25%+ in living cost makes less of a difference than subjective factors.

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan (cont) When I bought, I thought I was "overpaying", but was happy to pay that price as cost of admission.
· In my town, when meeting people, "owners" are assumed to stick around more, and renters less so. Easier to invest in relationships if someone will likely be around.

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan · Owning becomes cheaper and cheaper the longer you stay. Your mortgage doesn't increase, rent will. So owning has an embedded option so that your cost of living won't increase — pays off over the long term more.
@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan · US mortgages are weird in that they also have an option to re-finance. So, if today's interest rate turns out to be bad, you can always cut costs later.

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan (this is basically never factored into spreadsheet analysis)

· Owning made me feel rooted in a place more than I've ever felt before. For me, this was a very good thing. I invested more in my neighborhood, town, and relationships. I was "here".

@MilanGriffes @simonsarris @_StevenFan · Walkability within 5 minutes mattered more than I thought it would. There's a trail head 2 blocks away that I go to ~100 times a year. I would ~basically never drive there.
· Neighbors mattered more than I thought they would.

@Meaningness @kareem_carr My theory: most modern web programming is writing glue code, which is amenable to the sorts of things LMs can help with. For some roles, the bar isn't all that high, and compensation was high because so much money has been in tech.

@Meaningness @kareem_carr Yeah, fair. Though, I'm pretty convinced that the current LM transformer approach will continue to work well in domains such as code writing.

I was intensely skeptical ("it can do what *I* do professionally!") until seeing someone use it to do quite-non-trivial things.

@alangrow I suspect...

- What they trained on. It also can't compose music well.
- Trainability. Code is lexical. If you wanted a LM that did math symbolic manipulation, I wouldn't be surprised if it's possible (but so can Mathematica)

But if I had to give an earnest guess:

@alangrow Coding is: glue, implementing well-known patterns, and algorithmically solving "open" problems (i.e., closer to *formulating* problems themselves within the landscape of what's implementable with code).

@alangrow LMs can do the first two (and over time, better and better), and hardly the last one. I'd be wrong if you could show a LM solving a problem in a way that requires "novel insight".

Admittedly, that is a moving goal post. Ugh. Maybe always will be, and skeptics will keep moving it

@alangrow Junior programmers do a lot of glue and sometimes implement well-known algos. Copilot can do this. To the extent that is quite impressive!

For a certain level of sophistication, this looks like "insight". (This indeed does impress!) https://t.co/yRWjtlhJ4C

@alangrow Senior programmers do that, and also have insights into problems. Most often, when it's not that novel of a problem. Occasionally, Copilot does something that resembles that, to the extent that 'pretending' to be able to do X is close enough to doing X.

@alangrow Similar problem space: ChatGPT can explain deontology vs consequentialism, but (I predict) unable to exhibit "reason" for trivial logic problems that are formulated cleverly to trick how LMs are designed. And especially if it has never seen it before.
@alangrow Examples to illustrate my point. Here's the Prisoner's Dilemma, but where both defecting means no jail for either. Whoops! https://t.co/BZUH0mPoX5

@alangrow Here's the lier/truth-teller riddle, but where all you have to do is ask *any* question with a known answer. It doesn't know that, so tries to outwit them, making a logic error in the process. https://t.co/Yonj4zg9ZQ
@alangrow Here's a nonsensical riddle, where it misses that the prompt itself is wrong (both are wrong), and instead offers an incorrect factorization anyways. https://t.co/4syF9PDE7C
@alangrow Whatever aspect of cognition that makes those "easy" for humans is missing from current-gen LMs. Mathematics has a lot of that.

@alangrow FWIW, ChatGPT can indeed do decently impressive counterfactual "reasoning" that would compete with many grade-school aged students.

Many of the ethics-in-tech issues are about *scale*, and not so much about first-principles right or wrong.

For example, magazines (targeted to audiences who self-select) vs algorithmic personalized feeds. They do the same thing—personalization—but the latter 100x "better".

It's the same "give a penny, take a penny" ethics that Office Space scaled. At small scales, many things just aren't that big of a deal.

At infinite scale, they're dystopian.
This is why it's hard to talk about whether something in tech is bad. In isolation, probably not. At its limit, most definitely.
It's been possible to hire cheap writers to churn out mindless SEO-optimized content. While bad, with language model cost approaching ~free, the problem becomes 100x worse.

In a post-scarcity world, attention is *the* valuable resource, and we're about to be assaulted from all fronts. There's no proof of work to AI-generated content, so generating it to take attention from others is free.

We will all pay the cost.

I'm confident that we'll come up with solutions—though not cheaply. Perhaps integrating proof of work into most things will help.

If you knew that I was trusted by others to make reasonable posts, and spent 10 minutes on this thread, perhaps it's more valuable to you.

And obviously, we can't stop it. It's as if car manufactures kept making cars faster and faster each year, because speed is fun, and there's no reasonable way to be like "maybe this isn't a good idea?"

Personally, I think it's one of those things that you can have an outcome governed by a system, without specific intentionality from a component of the system.

People in power naturally believe in systems that benefit them, because a mixture of (a) it's convenient, (b) explains why they're "good", (c) gives them more power, whether they realize it or not, (d) if not, power would rotate until it found a stable point that was self-reinforcing.

You *could* have intentional agency, but I don't think it's needed — you could have the result of sedation without anyone in particular pulling the strings.

Specific example: Democrats and Republicans are opposed, but also reinforce each other by being legible enemies. They create issues that culture "must" take a position on, collapsing complex reality on to a one-dimensional axis. I suppose it's possible that secret meetings occur between the parties to agree on what issues will be "issues", but I find it much more likely that they both exist in a happy Nash equilibrium.

Evoking @remixeverything a bit, I see a difference between conspiracy theories and conspiracies.

I'm a bit more conservative in definitions, in that I do think information about conspiracies can be weaponized as a "conspiracy theory" (thus, dismissing it). But there's a useful pattern: over time, as more information is known, conspiracy theories stay muddy and require leaps, while conspiracies get clearer. Certainly this time window could be long, and this is primarily just a guide.

But in the class of faked moon landing, 9/11 inside job, flat earth, chem trails, etc, I do think exposure to understanding memetic ideas is helpful in thinking more clearly. You can see how ideas become optimized for replication rather than truth.

My issue with Enneagrams (and other similar tests) are that they're descriptive, not prescriptive, but often used as if they're prescriptive.

It's *always* possible to statistically cluster people by attributes, and then have associated stereotypes — those attributes will have correlative causes, influences, fears, beliefs, and life impacts.

But, it's entirely reasonable for the clustering to fail for some people or attributes to be situational. And then even if someone falls into a cluster fairly solidly, the causes/influences/fears/beliefs/impacts may miss.

I've known multiple people who are very into Enneagrams (including professionally as counselors who use them) that peg me solidly in a certain type with high certainty, but they disagree with each other. It's because I know them in different contexts. In some contexts, I'm always the one pushing for adventure. In others, as someone with a big heart. In others, as career-driven. Etc.

Not surprisingly, I get different results over time and between different tests. I read the descriptions, and most definitely see resonances, depending on where I am in life and what's been going on. But also huge misses.

It's more like "X mostly correlates/describes me right now" than "I am an X". And for whatever reason, many personality tests don't seem to offer correlations that are all that descriptive or helpful for me. Most of my strongest types have limiting subconscious beliefs / fears that I'm positive I don't have.

Because there's so many clusters + wings + triads + healthy/average/unhealthy, the model will most definitely hit in some ways.

My steelman for why they're helpful to many people is similar to someone getting an ADHD diagnosis. Having the label can be liberating because it comes with an identity and strategies to thrive, along with pitfalls and things to look out for. And perhaps it's the *ideal* tool for many people to understand themselves and orient towards healthy expressions of their personality.

Solutions to immigration must balance humanitarian needs, economic benefit (of immigrants and existing residents), and cultural impacts.

We should be selective about who can immigrate, taking into account occupation, criminal history, and educational attainment.

Immigration policies should favor people who will likely assimilate and contribute economically.

It should be easy for low-risk immigrants to legally enter and leave a country based on dynamic labor market needs.

Low-skill immigrants should have enforced labor protections.

Citizens / churches / non-profits should be able to sponsor immigrants, providing a built-in existing community for new immigrants.

Countries should strategically allocate immigrants to areas that would see the most economic benefit.

Immigrant legal status should not be tied to one specific employer, to prevent abuse. However, their legal status can be tied to having a productive job.

There should be programs to match low-skill labor to employer needs.

Issues I've found, assuming genuine content to begin with:

1. What's meaningful for one person may not be for another
2. Various pedagogical styles resonate with people differently
3. Engagement with content matters. Easy to not get much from a course without sufficient effort (Coursera discovered this)
4. Hard to judge how good a reviewer is. I've seen people be floored by some pretty benign courses

What I've found works: chatting with people who took a class and hear their story. See if they seem to be in a similar place and value similar things.

There's a trend in art to blur between subject/object, questioning authenticity itself. Is Bo Burnham actually depressed? How much of The Rehearsal was real? Jerrod Carmichael's Rothaniel, James Acaster's Repertoire.

Just finished Jury Duty, a show with 11 fake jurors… https://t.co/LozGLtlx2k

and 1 real person, who doesn't know everyone else is fake. He thinks they're all being followed by a documentary crew, and the actors run through various shenanigans.

It felt 15 years too late. It was Punk'd, filmed in the style of Arrested Development / The Office.

It's a postmodern take on metamodernism. They blur subject/object by mixing scripted segments with ones who's entire plot motivation was to watch the real person squirm. But there's no underlying earnestness.

There's only one character that matters, and he has no development.

He was casted to be likable, and they succeeded. But it's the same can-you-believe-he-thinks-this-is-real humor as Punk'd.

Overall, they missed *why* artists are blurring subject/object. Why authenticity feels at stake, in response to growing up very online.

I have a bit of a brand of being anti-podcast (vs audiobooks, keeping the same format) due to lack of proof of work and proclivity towards insight entertainment.

Coming around that they're solving a different thing: breadth first vs depth first.

Books cost the author and team minimally 100x the time it takes to consume them. Every word is edited, refined. It's a better distillation into ideas than a quick interview can do. Depth first.

i.e., you'll learn more about health reading @PeterAttiaMD's Outlive than 10 podcasts

Valuable podcasts for me have been to give a taste of someone's work, shuffled through the random interests of the interviewer. So it's not proper "start here" / "best of", but approaches that.

The failure mode for me was listening to hours++ of breadth without going deep.

It gives the illusion of learning. And if what you want is entertainment, they work well.

So I've been seeking out interviews with people I want to learn more about as entry points, and then going deep as relevant.

All of this is to say: @andy_matuschak on @dwarkesh_sp's podcast is wonderful, and if you haven't gone breadth first into his work, I recommend it!

@RobertHaisfield Send 1k tokens to GPT, get 2k back. You pay for those 1k and 2k, which feels okay because that correlates strongly with OpenAI’s costs and the value of this single API call.

Wrap it in a service that charges a percentage margin above OpenAI (perhaps, for fancy prompt engineering…

Send 1k tokens to GPT, get 2k back. You pay for those 1k and 2k, which feels okay because that correlates strongly with OpenAI’s costs and the value of this single API call.

Wrap it in a service that charges a percentage margin above OpenAI (perhaps, for fancy prompt engineering or their product’s UI). That feels weird, because the product’s value is far less correlated with the number of tokens used.

Makes more sense to just charge $0.01 or whatever per time it’s used, instead of counting tokens.

Though real margin comes from amortizing across specific requests and charging based on output value. Let’s say a prompt/response costs $0.05 to OpenAI, but the product’s prompting or UI makes the output worth a lot more. (Example: writing marketing taglines.) then just charge what the market would bear for the end product.

Randomness that you observe (ex: an app crashes randomly) is often due to other variables.

Yeah but you can't change an industry over night. Nearly every bank was short interest rates without hedges.

We don't disagree, but another perspective:

In a weird way, it's sort of working as expected. Avoiding m2m avoids solvency issues by pretending they don't exist. If the reason for solvency issues are interest rate changes, this is—in the weird way—kind of reasonable. Cheap loans are far less likely to default (the borrower is up a lot!), and mortgages do usually get paid back. So treating a $1m loan as down $300k is weird if there's no real reason to believe it won't be paid back. It's down just because of interest rate exposure.

If they're because of degraded performance (everyone's laid off, defaults are coming), then it's far less reasonable. Separating these two is difficult, but there is a belief that market price may not be "fair".

Also, mortgages in general have this feature, the other way. There is no margin call for borrowers, even if the underlying economic realities have changed. This is massively valuable.

Overall, I agree though. The solution is requiring banks to mark to market, thus implicitly requiring them to hedge. Let a professional risk taker take that risk, not depositors / the fed.

Yeah, I don't think cultural reversion is possible in general. The move to atomize the individual solved real problems, but opened new ones.

We're ~probably better off, but have minimal defenses against current issues. examples: attention economy (we don't have an aversion to extracting attention), polluting the commons (ecologically, but also in any way I can offload costs externally, including lying), loss of communal trust, tribal inclusiveness.

I personally lean towards thinking we need better emergent cultural systems that give this form of holistic support. And not sure how to get there. But I did move to a small town to tap into it, and that worked.

Many companies that want non-determinism use their own form of string-based ID.

YouTube video IDs are random, but shorter than UUID (and not hex).

Fairly common to encrypt an internal autoinc ID in a compact way, so externally it’s an opaque string, but edge services can convert back to numerical ID cheaply.

You can do something similar if you want to preserve approximate ordering. Construct a float that’s mostly time based, use some bits for the machine, and use some bits for machine-local autoinc. Guarantees approx time ordering, without distributed coordination. Can be converted to base64 or whatever to be more compact.

The core issue is that modern medicine works by coding around reference ranges. There's no great way of knowing what a range should be, so they're usually just set based on saying x% of the population is "normal".

1. Directly in line what you experienced, *anything* in normal is seen as fine, even if you're young and quickly headed out of normal. And, the doctors have seen way worse, so someone who's borderline is ¯\_(ツ)_/¯.
2. Many people are in various degrees of sick, so this is a bad approach to begin with. If 40% will get diabetes, for instance, then that's not a good reference.
3. Ranges use populations. Better hope the population even remotely represents you. Often age, sex, genetics, etc can vastly change what's normal — each person has some degree of idiosyncrasy.

So yeah, most definitely advocate for yourself and don't blindly trust panel ranges.

Two lenses that I like when thinking about creativity and information diets:

1. Creativity is everywhere, but you need to be a willing vessel. (The Creative Act and Big Magic have this idea)
2. You get good at things you do a lot. Creating space for creativity reenforces that, and it becomes easier.

People like us struggle with this—creating space / low information—because we have sensitive "interesting" neural circuitry. We really like learning and finding interesting content, so have difficulty limiting our intake. I first realized this ~10 years ago when I noticed how much I'd scroll sites like Reddit, feeling like I was learning and spending time usefully, but getting nearly nothing out of it. "Interesting porn"

Best progress I've made is being intentional about where information comes from. Slow content > infinite algo feeds. For example, I use an ad blocker to remove all recommended videos on YouTube, and basically exclusively consume by curating subscriptions into playlists, then working through those over time. This turns YouTube into a site that can be "caught up on", rather than always has more to offer.

I typically get more from books / audiobooks than podcasts. Occasionally a podcast is good, but interview-style podcasts typically have low information density AND low curation power. Books have low density, but every word was edited.

Tactically, I often listen to audiobooks while walking outside. Whenever I have an interesting idea, I take a voice note, which then gets transcribed and summarized. It lets me freely just talk, instead of trying to refine the verbal presentation (writing is too slow!).

One last idea: create a place for things to land. Several years ago, I started noticing metaphors that meant a lot to me — they seemed to give more and move over time. So, I started collecting them. Once I had a place for interesting metaphors, I started to notice more. And then, once I found a piece of content about one specific metaphor, I could easily add it — building up my understanding over time.

Similar: a notepad for jokes you think of, or business ideas. Create a landing place for things you want more of, and then more will come.

I agree about K:Na, but think statins are good ROI for a lot of people. ApoB is just that bad.

My perspective:
- ASCVD risk is about exposure × time. People don't start early enough, especially with pharmacological interventions.
- Statins are safe. I've read all the people who claim they're not. Risk adjusted return matters — they aren't good fits for everyone, but do save lives. Same for most drugs.
- Statins are not enough for standard Americans. Slamming down ApoB (not necessarily an ideal goal for everyone) needs solid diet, exercise, and pharmacological interventions.
- There's tons of statins. If one gives side effects, try another.
- High dose statin is dumb. Supermajority of benefit comes from first 5mg. We have other levers now.
- PSK9i's, Ezetimibe, Bempedoic acid are probably better for many people (or a combination), but are a lot more expensive.

I also have family history of ASCVD, and had high ApoB in my late 20s with a decent diet. It's top 10% now.

These are actually far more powerful than most people realize. Working on a guide how to build custom experts — more than just a clever prompt. Comes down to:

1. Connecting to "good" knowledge
2. Designing conversational style, tone, output, topic, etc. (setting a "vibe") https://t.co/meNpimU7op

For example, let's say you wanted Richard Feynman. You need to both plug it to relevant physics/science information, but also match his style and approach.

Knowledge is easy, but has significant limitations. I figured them out, which makes it easier to design around them.

Vibe is harder. Emulating a person requires distilling a lot of their content, looking for themes / tone / approach. And what can do this? GPT.

I fed a book-length of text and had GPT write a vibe guide. It beat my custom prompt hands-down.

Read that paper. The article is particularly bad, but the paper shows a weaker case as well:

· Their analysis is based on 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles, so we're considering the middle chunk, not the tails.
· Higher achievers have a limit to how much they can improve. They model using log odds, but there's an obvious pareto impact here — left to their own devices, a kid already at mastery would just move on and stack another learning curve on top. (Differences compound)
· Standard deviation of learning rate explodes (>2x difference) between 25th and 75th percentile learning rate bucketed students. i.e., the "best" learners behaved more similarly, the "worst" learners diverged.

Plagiarism as an academically unforgivable crime always kind of confused me. In a composition class, if someone was lazy and wrote a really bad sentence with bad arguments, that's just a bad grade.

If someone was lazy and copied fifteen words verbatim from a source, but with…

Plagiarism as an academically unforgivable crime always kind of confused me. In a composition class, if someone was lazy and wrote a really bad sentence with bad arguments, that's just a bad grade.

If someone was lazy and copied fifteen words verbatim from a source, but with quotes and a citation, that's ~fine. If they left those off, that's ~unforgivable. But the specific assembly of the words is the least interesting thing they're learning to do! If they need to borrow the word assembly, then, indeed, that is evidence of non-mastery.

If they're lazy and take a big conceptual idea from somewhere else, but rephrase entirely in their own words, then it kind of depends how big the idea is whether someone would care. APA citations don't really capture what was taken, which means taking a big, complex idea and not citing is actually easier. In fact, really big ideas are hard to come by, and often are mashups of a lot of other big ideas. If I riffed about meaning, there's no way I'd not be borrowing big ideas from David Chapman and John Vervaeke — I've basically internalized them to where I'm not even sure where they're specifically from.

I remember having ideas, then trying to backfill citations so every idea was "supported". Since there's no way to cite "I went on a long walk and this seems right to me", or "this is from the nebulous combination of my life experience", you have to come up with something. Citing things make sense when you're citing specific facts (you want a custody chain of facts in the sciences), and if you leave one off, it's obvious. So the real issue is in the liberal arts, where many citations are more conceptual.

The danger zone seems to be taking a small-ish idea, and violating proof-of-work and honesty norms and presenting as one's own. It's kind of a bad crime, though, because replacing the borrowed content with bad content or a quote solves the immediate problem. Functionally, the student is just robbing themselves by not learning to do the independent-thought thing.

It only functionally matters because of credentials/degrees/etc, which are proxies for "I did a hard thing and earned it through hard work, not freeloading on others". Outsiders don't have a way of verifying all the work I did to get my degree, so use the degree as a proxy of "work was done", co-signed by a university that puts their reputation on the line.

So, we end up with absolute truth norms that we have to take very seriously, to keep a proof-of-work system working. Fine. But, I knew plenty of people who found easier ways of getting their degree (easy majors, choosing easier professors, working with other students, taking classes again, strategically dropping classes at midterms, etc). The proxy was already kind of broken, and GPT/etc just further break it. So, maybe this whole delegated proxy system isn't as useful as it was before?

There's two ways of doing science:

· Science Inc. Institutions, incrementalism, reputation. This is the beat that relentlessly marches forward. It slowly inflates our bubble of understanding, at the cost of spending incredible resources towards bureaucracy and cargo culting.… https://t.co/yldOkpziUD

There's two ways of doing science:

· Science Inc. Institutions, incrementalism, reputation. This is the beat that relentlessly marches forward. It slowly inflates our bubble of understanding, at the cost of spending incredible resources towards bureaucracy and cargo culting. It's optimized for predictable outcomes, which sometimes means "no" outcomes. However, it keeps the pumps of progress flowing.

· Cowboy Science. Shooting from the hip, hype, wild — in terms of asymmetric outcomes, and often epistemics. They internalize that Greatness Cannot Be Planned. It's a random walk, and sometimes you strike gold. Frequently wrong, but since Science is a strong-link problem, it doesn't matter in practical terms.

Science Inc hates Cowboy Science because they want to be them, but are constrained not to be. Occasionally someone from Science Inc gets enough clout to practice Cowboy Science, but it's rare. This leaves the eccentrics, who as a population care less about conformity and pleasing the crowds. They have more unusual beliefs, and can be object-level wrong. The disdain you see against people doing Cowboy Science is about conformity. Why, exactly, is it bad that a millionaire is working on a wild quest? Ah yes, because it isn't part of the monolith.

When you see Cowboy Science, think of them as explorers, purposely doing something way different than everyone else as a hope of getting out of local minimums. This doesn't mean they're right or should be directly followed, but it's a noble path.

Obviously deceitful—strictly, and superficially—but here's why I think it's not all that immoral.

The deceit is in the communication "there's high demand for this, act fast" when there may not be.

· It's low fidelity communication anyways. It may be actually in high demand, but there's many flyers, and you're seeing a fresh one.
· Everyone knows that trick, so it's not actually deceptive in practice. Everyone's ~basically on the same page. It's a little socially acceptable dance that everyone knows the moves for.
· So, it must be doing something else as well. It also communicates "it's okay to act on this, don't worry about ruining the flyer by taking a tear-off". This is actually honest, and I suspect is just as relevant as the potential deceit.

@a_m_mastroianni When we see things like "scientists report that curcumin decreases inflammation" (or whatever), there's a few giant leaps happening. Who are the scientists, and what are their motives? What exactly did they study? Was it actual humans, or, like, mice? What, exactly, were they…

When we see things like "scientists report that curcumin decreases inflammation" (or whatever), there's a few giant leaps happening. Who are the scientists, and what are their motives? What exactly did they study? Was it actual humans, or, like, mice? What, exactly, were they looking for? What duration? What side effects did they screen for? (i.e., lowering "inflammation" doesn't matter if it also increases, say, cancer) What body of literature does this agree with? How much do we know about an actual optimal dosing protocol? etc etc

Nearly all supplement research can't answer most of those, because it wasn't designed to. Nutritional science is typically looking in a very zoomed in subset of what people actually care about. People actually want to know "should I take curcumin?", which is massively more nuanced. Depends on what it typically does for people, what needs to be fixed/improved, etc.

So, perhaps nutrition science is broken, but perhaps it's just the wrong tool for what people actually want.

Here's my wild idea: companies that make health claims must disclose what the claims actually mean, then post a bounty for replication failures.

The failures have to be solid (ie, evidence of no effect, not no evidence of effect), and disagreements go to expert arbitration. https://t.co/S7KPJecERb

Example: you sell red light therapy masks for skincare, and want to write marketing copy that resembles health claims. You fund or collect research showing whatever you want to show.

Same FDA/FCC laws as now, but there must be public info that pairs each health claim with…

Example: you sell red light therapy masks for skincare, and want to write marketing copy that resembles health claims. You fund or collect research showing whatever you want to show.

Same FDA/FCC laws as now, but there must be public info that pairs each health claim with specifically what they mean. Then, there'd also be a $1m bounty (can be bonded, I'm fine with markets for this risk) if someone can show no effect, same power as you used. If you used p<0.05, they can too, but null hypothesis becomes that there is an effect equal to the size you report.

Arbitration clears challenge study design. For example, if the claim is "reduces fine wrinkles by 30%", the health claim support page would explain exactly what that means. If anything's vague (like, what is a fine wrinkle? how to measure reduction?), then arbitration board may approve a different, reasonable way of quantifying the claims.

"Wait, this means we'll then have to dig to see what each health claim actually means?"
No, same laws about health claims as now. This is in addition. In practice, this is a strong link problem. All it takes is someone doubting and challenging the claims to make it too costly.

Desired outcome would be to have more disclosure about what the health claims actually mean, and a way for bad claims to be economically expensive to make.

My wild guess: Elon wanted to show video creators that X is a viable content distribution platform, both in terms of engagement and revenue, so he wanted to make sure the post had incredibly high distribution.

They didn't have a mechanism to force high distribution, or recognized that just dropping it in everyone's feed would be immediately problematic. However, they have the Ad platform, which does both desired effects: artificially increases distribution, while trying to do so in a targeted manner.

The only thing to clean up is the "Ad" banner, which was probably much easier to selectively remove than trying to implement a big newsfeed change. Yet, it's a leaky abstraction, with other ways of seeing that it was an Ad.

Scientific bounties / challenge awards have a long history of promoting causes and solving hard problems.

We should be doing a lot more of these, working on an essay about how this would work.

Until then, some of my favorite science bounties: https://t.co/wE2QHbFnJY

Orteig Prize (1919) for the first non-stop flight between New York City and Paris. Commercial air travel didn't exist, so private industry wasn't trying to solve for long-haul flights. Charles Lindbergh won 8 years later.

Ansari X Prize (2004) for the first non-governmental launch of a reusable manned spacecraft into space twice within two weeks.

To create a massive space industry, you need to show private industry can execute separate from governments and cheaply.

Ansari X Prize (2004) for the first non-governmental launch of a reusable manned spacecraft into space twice within two weeks.

To create a massive space industry, you need to show private industry can execute separate from governments and cheaply.

Napoleonic Food Preservation Prize (1810), for a method to preserve food for Napoleon's army. When you're mobile, you need food that preserves well. This led to the invention of canning. Solving this has obvious spillover effects to everyone else.

Donald Knuth's reward check, where famed the computer scientist and mathematician wrote (very small) checks to people who found errors in his work. The checks became valued prizes, and found many, many errors.

The Netflix Prize (2009), to improve recommendation algorithms. They didn't actually end up using the winning entry (!). More importantly, it brought in massive publicity and attention from the data science and machine learning communities, and encouraged several similar prices.
The Longitude Act (1714), to determine a ship's longitude at sea. This was a huge deal — ships would often end up 100s of miles off their targets.

@uberstuber Unfortunately, no one is truly on "your side". Your realtor wants any deal done, sellers obviously are against you, etc. So advocate for yourself, and be willing to ask a lot of dumb questions.

The inspection is critical, but also will be incomplete — their job isn't to tell you…

Unfortunately, no one is truly on "your side". Your realtor wants any deal done, sellers obviously are against you, etc. So advocate for yourself, and be willing to ask a lot of dumb questions.

The inspection is critical, but also will be incomplete — their job isn't to tell you directly "this is a great house" or not. Assume there will be plenty of things that they won't find. For example, our house has stucco that goes into the ground. They casually mentioned that people wouldn't do that anymore, because water can wick up behind it. We weren't able to get a straight answer about how big of a deal that was. The inspection will be the starting point — you then may want to call a plumber or electrician to check something. We dodged a $6k cost by finding a hole in the sewage line that the inspector didn't find.

Your lender will want to sell you points. Unless you think interest rates won't go down and you'll be there a long time, you probably won't want to do it. There are calculators online, so you can see if it makes sense, but realize that the lender makes money on this (i.e., expected value isn't positive).

If you can be picky, care a lot about the neighborhood. Many of these people will live near you for a long time. Location crossed wit your own preferences matter a ton.

If you don't own them, buy a bunch of tools so you can fix stuff yourself. For cordless power tools, pick a specific brand that you'll stick with, because batteries cost a lot. I picked Milwaukee, but there's several good alternatives. Don't buy one-off tools based on the best deal, you'll end up needing batteries for several platforms.

YouTube has videos for basically every home maintenance task.

Early on, find a plumber, electrician, etc that you like. Easier to have one already than trying to find one in an emergency.

Start looking at homes well before you are serious, because you'll get better at shopping for homes. You'll know better what you want, etc.

You may get significant discounts for auto insurance by lumping it with homeowners insurance. Our auto is actually free because we were insuring two houses (cheaper car, discount was more than the policy).

Mortgage is the least you'll pay, yada yada, but it's also the most you'll pay except for maintenance. i.e., the payoff vs renting is far in the future when inflation has made rents higher. Many rent vs own calculators don't really consider what it'll be like in 20+ years being able to pay well under market, assuming your home value didn't collapse.

On the super long term, house prices can't outpace inflation (specifically, wages), because exponential growth can't continue forever. The past few decades has been unusual, with prices propped up by declining interest rates / 30 yr mortgages / etc. I can't predict what will happen with prices, but understand we've been in historically unusual times. Don't go in assuming prices will appreciate.

Shop for mortgages (you can apply to many within 30 days and only be docked once on your credit report), but understand that if you're talking to a broker, they're just reselling the mortgage and taking a spread. i.e., most mortgages are fairly competitive price wise.

If there's anything weird about your situation, a bank (vs mortgage broker) can actually underwrite and carry the mortgage. Not sure if interest rates being up has changed this, but in 2020 we went with a bank, who was able to underwrite a Jumbo mortgage with better terms than the brokers could.

The house buying practice is oddly impersonal, due to the way agents work. We only met the seller once, it was kind of weird. But I understand why. People get weirdly personal about this stuff.

Be smart about it, but it may be beneficial to be clear about what you want. We were buying a home in a place we didn't live and weren't planning on coming back to until closing, and we asked for a super long closing timeline. Turns out the seller wanted that too, but was too afraid to ask. So it's helpful to try to get exactly what you want.

@fortelabs In addition to payback period, a super important variable is to consider different cohorts — both by funnel, and over time.

By funnel: suppose half your customers come in for free, and half cost $540. This averages to your current stats, but means that if you poured more into…

In addition to payback period, a super important variable is to consider different cohorts — both by funnel, and over time.

By funnel: suppose half your customers come in for free, and half cost $540. This averages to your current stats, but means that if you poured more into paid acquisition, you'd be paying double what you think you should be.

It's useful to try to figure out what your free baseline is, and then if there's different funnel cohorts that exist. For example, one may be people who find you via YouTube — in this case, you can look at what it costs to acquire them. Separately, you may have other funnels.

Separating this gives you a much better picture. I knew of a company that was paying over double the LTV in a specific ad channel, but overall was averaging a decent LTV : CAC ratio, so kept doing it.

Similarly, consider how it changes over time. You may be saturating certain markets, and your CAC is trending up. Or, some funnels may get more efficient over time, with their CAC dropping.

Lastly, each funnel and time period may have different LTVs. For example, people coming from your book may have a lower LTV than people coming from YouTube. Or whatever.

Fundamentally, you're fighting against the strong force of averages obfuscating what reality looks like for different groups of people. All of this will help you understand where to invest more, and if different CACs are reasonable.

Continuing the riff... combining two of my favorite capitalist things (bounties, insurance markets).

What if companies sponsoring research (i.e., conflict of interest exists) go through peer review, but a part of this is underwriting for bounty payout risk.

Insurance price is set by a market (people bid). The underwriting prices insurance against bounties on this paper. To publish, the company pays to get bounty insurance.

Bounty $ offered + insurance price tells us all we need to know about market-assessed reliability.

Why the heck would a company do this?

In a world where peer review doesn't mean much, people should be increasingly skeptical about research with conflicts of interest. This allows companies and markets to negotiate about trustworthyness of research.

Makes sense. Yeah, consider:

f("hi")="ih", f("hello")="olleh", f("")=""

The simplest function that passes is 3 if statements. But that's obviously not what's desired, and most people could figure out that f(x) reverses a string.

Make it less trivial, though, and the inference gets harder. This sort of inference jump is what humans (via pattern matching and making contextual assumptions) are good at, vs LLMs.

An interesting workflow would be to describe a function, get it, and then add a unit test that currently fails that should pass, and the LLM would update the function. Sort of combines both approaches, letting you clearly give edge cases that should be considered.

Could you steelman why they’re so bad?

Totally understand that income/wealth doesn’t (in the positive or negative case) ensure investing sophistication. And it’s a bit nanny state that the government picks who’s allowed (while lotteries, buying penny stocks, casinos, etc are accessible).

But… I’m not convinced that venture is a uniquely great asset class (assume roughly fairly priced, as evidenced by angellist published returns), and have a strong prior that venture outperformance comes *from* sophistication (quality network, understanding tech, portfolio construction). All the while that it’s incredibly high variance. This belief is *after* an exceedingly strong tech bull market.

And throwing money towards venture funds is super high fee, and basically a tech momentum strategy (ie, crowds when things are already good into leveraged Nasdaq exposure, plus error from micro-cap factor).

Obviously there’s a counterfactual that someone could have thrown $10k into Uber and become financially secure though that, but (a) most people who could do that much are already accredited if diversified, (b) selection bias. Expected value neutral 100x returns are technically available in options, if we value the potential of wild returns.

What I’m getting at: in practice, are a lot of people negatively impacted by existing laws? Even assuming a young (not rich) tech worker (let’s assume sophisticated) who is deeply bullish in tech and their friend’s companies… it’s near impossible to invest enough to have meaningful returns. And in situations I’ve seen this, no one’s actually asking for accreditation proof.

What I’ve seen instead with public solicitation weakening is that the worst deals are public to micro checks, and the best deals are still done through relationships and networks. Obviously a broad generalization, but I’ve seen returns from public solicitation markets, and it’s abysmal.

I say this as someone who personally has a large tech portfolio. I’m just skeptical that in practice it’s really doing a big injustice. I’m sure you’ve thought a lot about this, so genuinely curious.
