My process to distill books/podcasts/etc into "guides" for large context large language models: https://t.co/LzpChaHhRE

1. Get raw text content from a book, podcast, lecture, etc. If it's audio, I use MacWhisper to generate a good transcript.

2. Pick your LLM. I'm using Claud Opus, which needs a premium subscription, but importantly does not charge based on usage.
This is important because—even though you'll get rate limited—large context usage would become prohibitively expensive quickly. I often have days that would cost ~$50 at retail API prices.

3. It's common that the content will be too large. You have 200k tokens total to work with. You can get a rough size of token count by using OpenAI's Tokenizer tool. If you have an epub/pdf, consider converting to markdown first using an online tool or pandoc.

If it still won't fit with at least 30k tokens to spare, then split up the source file into roughly equal chunks that will fit.

Then, you will feed the chunks in separate conversations, but always include the summary so far in later chunks.

4. Carefully prompt. Here's an example that includes half of a book. Notice how I coax it into a very long summary — we don't want to end up with just a few bullet points.

You also want to do as much "pre-computation" as possible, which I accomplish by asking for Q&As. https://t.co/M5Ceyj262O

I've found it's able to come up with fairly significant questions (and then, answer them) that weren't directly covered in the summary.

If loading in chunks, you'd create a new chat and use a similar prompt for the 2nd, but also include the prior summary output.

5. With all summary chunks (if multiple) combined, consider if there's any further synthesis that would be useful to pre-compute. This is basically doing a "let's think step by step", and reifying synthesis of big concepts so that later use is easier.

For example, for my Jungian…

5. With all summary chunks (if multiple) combined, consider if there's any further synthesis that would be useful to pre-compute. This is basically doing a "let's think step by step", and reifying synthesis of big concepts so that later use is easier.

For example, for my Jungian typography guide, I had it do things such as:
1. Give many examples of how a person of each type would live
2. Describe conflicts each type may find themselves in
3. Enumerating at least 5 questions for each type that help determine if this type is relevant for a person.
4. Describe in detail how to apply the model, as a guide written for a therapist.
6. You'll end up with a large file that distills many important ideas from the original text. For example, I used roughly 3 hours of podcast audio to produce a 10kb "guide". https://t.co/j9ypqMyxlu

7. In a new conversation converse with the guide directly. Example: a book about business development may create a good guide for strategy coaching, or a fiction book may create a guide that can be used to suggest great book club discussion questions.

More charts because the astrologer population average performance may be abysmal, but a few people are really good. So separate those out. (curious to see the distribution!)

Easier to win because the threshold seems *really* high. I’d be quite surprised if someone can do half right over a large sample. Since I’m sure you don’t want too much work for participants, 14 out of 20 seems safe for you, for instance (~0.01%). If you were willing to have a dynamic test duration, then you could get closer to half (or whatever) by having more charts for statistically successful readers.

Also, not sure if already covered, but make sure to have a bank of significantly more than one test’s worth of charts, so that any charts that are “easy” or “hard” (ie, readers tend to converge on similar readings which could be right or wrong by change) get averaged out across many tests.

You ask whether it's necessary (in my own words: is there something good…

For frameworks like this, I think they can be useful/functional without knowing how "true" they are. But, I do think the model works pretty well. On reflection, unhappiness does seem to be rooted in belief.

You ask whether it's necessary (in my own words: is there something good about unhappiness?). My first guess would be "motivation to change", but I'm not convinced that's actually true. Unhappiness is an attractor basin that creates a fog around and mutes everything. It's easier to change if you're able to think freely about the situation.

Example: say I have no friends, thus feel unhappy. Why? Perhaps I feel alone. Why is that bad? Well, perhaps it implies people don't like me — that I'm unlikable. That feeling may be a motivation to go make friends, but in practice, I suspect it hinders that more than helps. If I have a deep desire for friends, I don't need unhappiness to motivate me. I want it directly!

But what if people indeed don't like me, because they find me annoying? That hurts, because I may have a belief "people not liking me means I'm not worth anything". Another belief. Perhaps to get my desire, I need to consider life changes. Well, the unhappiness doesn't help — it muddies everything. I'm most motivated when I see the situation clearly, without beliefs that make it hard to change (my behavior loses friends, and I want to change in order to get friends).

So, I think not?

One clarification, which may or may not just be semantics: I don't think the model says that happiness is a choice*, per se. More like:

(continued...)

https://t.co/Y13iYr485D

1. unhappiness arises internally as a specific psychological strategy
2. it is rooted in beliefs about the world and self, connected to deeper desires
3. the beliefs are optional, and can be critiqued
4. keeping the desires doesn't require the unhappiness to exist

In effect,…

1. unhappiness arises internally as a specific psychological strategy
2. it is rooted in beliefs about the world and self, connected to deeper desires
3. the beliefs are optional, and can be critiqued
4. keeping the desires doesn't require the unhappiness to exist

In effect, it's offering a perspective that says that unhappiness is a strategy a lot of people use to be happy, and there's a far more direct and effective path available.

That said, everyone's unique and there are no psychological absolutes. We're certainly in the domain of "useful to some, no claims on Truth". I'm positive that there's other useful perspectives to hold—some that may even seem incompatible. Several people have cited somatic therapies, Core Transformation, etc as refinements.

* (I updated the section subtitle that was misleading)

@hariya108 Motivation can come from many places. You're right that many founders appear to use unhappiness as a motivation — about how the world is, their wealth, their power, etc.

However, the model claims that the unhappiness itself is optional. The underlying motivation ("I want to…

Motivation can come from many places. You're right that many founders appear to use unhappiness as a motivation — about how the world is, their wealth, their power, etc.

However, the model claims that the unhappiness itself is optional. The underlying motivation ("I want to change the world", "I want to be rich", etc) can exist by itself without cloudiness. And, the unhappiness likely reduces how much the motivation can express itself.

So, just because a lot of founders are unhappy doesn't mean the unhappiness was required to be a founder. What's needed, either way, are core desires.

Separately, there's an outcome — would I be just as happy if the company fails? Well, no, if I have beliefs that attach meaning to the failure. My first startup (mostly) failed, and I think back on the experience as a wonderful growing and learning experience. As in, if I could go back, I'd do it again. I don't have any unhappiness about it.

Not being unhappy doesn't mean I'm impartial to the outcome. It's usually more enjoyable to have success than failure, most definitely. But taking the failure path doesn't mean I'd have to be unhappy about it.

@danshipper Democracy in America by de Tocqueville was published. In our current sensibilities, we might think "oh, of course US was a democracy" but that wasn't well-established! (US was a representative republic)

Instead—my own interpretation—it noticed the stark difference between…

Democracy in America by de Tocqueville was published. In our current sensibilities, we might think "oh, of course US was a democracy" but that wasn't well-established! (US was a representative republic)

Instead—my own interpretation—it noticed the stark difference between emerging US sensibilities and French class structure that made the "irresistible revolution" that was starting obvious to de Tocqueville. We weren't there yet, but it was clear how individualism was taking hold.

He credited the Puritans as the seed, which is OUR modern interpretation (ie, they came for religious freedom, equality between all people, etc). This wasn't necessarily true at the time — Puritans wanted freedom for their own practices, but only that.

This text was influential, both because it encouraged the spread of these ideas in Europe, and planted the seed of governance exceptionalism in the US. He nailed a LOT about what would happen: individualism, tyranny of the majority, material consumerism.

@danshipper I also find the interplay between Protestant revivals ↔ transcendentalism fascinating, which was in full swing. It's an interesting study in history because the West has waves of rationalism vs romantic thought that oscillate. We're in a transition now.

imho: doesn’t exist. Certifications work when a domain is well understood, both on the demand (what do we want people to know) and education (how do we teach it) sides.

They’re demanded by people who don’t actually understand the domain so need a proxy, or when a very commoditized skill is needed. Often, what’s known is a process, not anything that could be pointed at and said “see, I did it”. Think windows network management, cutting hair, workplace safety.

ML is basically none of these. When I hired ML engineers, there wasn’t even a pretense that a proxy for knowledge would exist. It’d be entirely about what you’ve done, how well you can solve a toy problem, how you think about solving open problems, etc. And the specific things I needed may be unrelated to what another company wants.

So, your strategy may depend on what you want to do. A lot of actual applied ML is wrapping, fine-tuning, etc existing models. Relatively few are actually writing the code that trains SoTA models — far more are building products, scaling infra, etc. If instead you want to do research, it’s difficult (not impossible) to get into without academic exposure.

If you want to aim for median “ML Engineer” sort of roles, you can probably self teach, create some interesting things to continue learning, and get decent at ML interviews. Fortunately there’s so much free content in open repos, books, and videos so this is doable. Once you’ve done ML work professionally, it’ll become a lot easier to move jobs towards more things you enjoy.

@eriktorenberg The point about 4 year grants being better is super true — if it's early stage, the built-in assumption is that the equity grows by multiples or goes to zero.

Meaning: only doing yearly grants means company success actually rachets the future grants _down_ significantly. Another…

The point about 4 year grants being better is super true — if it's early stage, the built-in assumption is that the equity grows by multiples or goes to zero.

Meaning: only doing yearly grants means company success actually rachets the future grants _down_ significantly. Another way to think about it: an employee can always quit if the company's prospects are dim, and will likely stay if their equity package grew in value a lot. So, 4 year grant (vs 1 year) provides significantly more potential upside for employees, because they can behave strategically.

If they notice this, they'll strongly prefer larger grant earlier on. If they don't, then doing 1 yr grants will feel similar. Obviously, 1 yr grants are better for the company, for all the opposite reasons, except for retention.

Patreon, private Substack, etc — though downside is that normal unit there is paying over time, vs one-time pre-sale. If you want discrete, have an annual cost that's reasonable, or just start with Gumroad and figure out community stuff later.

Suggestion: avoid pay what you want. Or set it to be at least 2x the final cost. Paradoxically, higher cost selects for people who want to will this into existence, and think of it less of a purchase.

Concept, imho, should be: here's money to will it into existence; try your best, but zero expectations of what comes out or when. If someone would rather buy it when I know when/if/what it is, have them wait.

Does a few things:
1. more money from fewer people
2. clearer expectations
3. (ideally) higher quality, distilled feedback

A @movebettersam story:

My wife, a trail runner, broke her leg skiing 16 months ago. Even with hours of PT a week, her recovery plateaued last August.

Running 5 miles was near impossible. She was worried she’d never fully recover. https://t.co/2PpPGONnnF https://t.co/6mWCTpDfiz

For context, we live in a very outdoors-centric area. Running was her outlet, her social group. And PTs here are actually good!

She was worried she’d need another surgery, or that she’d always have knee and hip pain.

Sam took a call with her. Immediately he knew her core issue.

“We can fix that in less than a month”

He created a routine for her. Stretching, targeted strength training, rolling, flossing. Everything detailed with videos showing the mechanism. Rolling should be slow!

It was a lot, but near immediately, things started to change. A month later, she ran 13 miles on trail.

This year, she’s run Grand Canyon rim to rim and many long runs. 14ers, big climbs/descents, stacking distance. https://t.co/NKjf2YuCp2

Remember, she had been doing hours of in person PT a week. But, even with competent therapists, things weren’t looking good.

Sam is very good at what he does.

She still does the routine regularly. It’s actually increased both of our awareness of long term preventive care — so that we can do big stuff for a long time.

Wild this isn’t standard medical care as people age. You might not have to live with all those nagging pains!

The source of her specific pain was related to over-activation of some muscles (always engaged, over working), and under-activation of others (weren't used while she was on crutches, atrophied).

Resolving was about rolling very slowly to send clear signals from muscle to brain, re-establishing symmetry, and building strength through range of motion.

I'm sure there were other mechanical things that needed to happen, but that was the big area.

Additionally, she didn't have a good understanding what kinds of pain were okay (push through to expand ability), and what were not good. Sam identified her bones/ligaments were good, so doing higher intensity exercise was actually good — pushing through some new barriers that she had.

Her in-person PT was a lot more conservative. Don't push through pain, ice inflammation, etc. I don't know what they focused on, but she said the stretches and exercises that Sam suggested were different. Their model was that her IT band was rubbing against the plate (may or may not have been true), and that needed time to resolve.

filter:follows

within_time:48h

This was surprising (volume of n-sphere → 0 as n increases), so I played with the math to develop an intuition.

What clicked: higher dimensions add constraints to the n-sphere (another coordinate that must satisfy the sphere equation)

another weird example in next tweet:

Consider the shell of the outer 1% of a n-sphere. In low dimensions, it contains very little of the volume. But as you increase dimensions, nearly all is within that outer shell! https://t.co/ECBIcDPe9X
This is dependent on radius. As that increases, you can contain more "stuff" within the sphere, so the volume increases for longer before dimensionality taking over.

The way I've tried to rectify the tension is…

Been thinking about this as well, because I know what the Buddhists are pointing at, but also people who I respect talk about "true self" and "essence" as if there's a foundation / floor (and not in a "pure awareness" style).

The way I've tried to rectify the tension is directionally:

1. no-self view: self is an appearance, selfing is the process of patterns
2. psychological view: we have a lot of patterns built up that rely on personas, stable identity, ego, etc. those are worth looking at! as you unpeel them, you get closer and closer to more fundamental patterns (a good clue are the sorts of things you enjoyed as a kid). indeed, these are also just patterns, but practically, are probably better approximations about what may bring about joy, engagement, etc for each person. as a helpful tip, it may be useful to find and cultivate them.

So, from beginner's mind, they have minimal conflict (both pointing in the "lifting limited concepts of self" direction). Then, at the limit, individuation is best thought of as a process (not state) that is non-fixed, and enacts where meaning/purpose for each person is arising from.

Something like that?

Broadly, popular interest in science is resonance with an aesthetic — one related to using numbers, making causal claims, etc.

One of the reasons Huberman is so popular. Confidence, correctly articulated technical words, the aura of understanding. https://t.co/w0zxc9IIo4

Broadly, popular interest in science is resonance with an aesthetic — one related to using numbers, making causal claims, etc.

One of the reasons Huberman is so popular. Confidence, correctly articulated technical words, the aura of understanding.

This aesthetic is a part of western culture — we want to understand mechanisms! Even our woo and spirituality embraces it.

Everyone cites studies and uses technical language, even if entirely wrong. Christianity even has the rationalist technique of apologetics.

While science is precise and uses technical language, a particularly memetic version uses this to steamroll someone's critical thinking abilities.

@jimseven nails this — if we slip and let this aesthetic dominate, we can miss some pretty obvious questions.

It matters because (a) it cheapens what we actually know, (b) gives confidence to what we don't.

Sounding confident and being confident are different. What's the optimal morning routine? Open question, but definitely depends on "for what purpose?"

One of the reasons I'm excited by the outside-in approach of @a_m_mastroianni. Arm the rebels.

Yeah, some science requires particle colliders, but plenty is just laying out there for curious people to play with. And that is some of the most useful for everyday life.

(A part of this is an appreciation for James Hoffman and others who ask "wait, really?". So many sounds-like-facts enter our heads without us questioning. I'm bummed that I didn't think to test this!)

@jmitch Many laws are intentionally overlapping. An obvious example is how conspiracy can be a separate crime from the actual end crime. The theory is that each may apply to a different aspect of a crime (outcome, intent, motivation, etc). This allows discretion in cases where we want to…

Many laws are intentionally overlapping. An obvious example is how conspiracy can be a separate crime from the actual end crime. The theory is that each may apply to a different aspect of a crime (outcome, intent, motivation, etc). This allows discretion in cases where we want to treat a crime slightly differently based on the particulars (ie, we may decide racially motivated murders are particularly bad).

It also lets us fill in gaps. For example, the federal government may feel there’s gaps within different state laws about murder, so wants coverage for certain situations.

Double jeopardy still applies, so charges often get “merged”.

@DefenderOfBasic @hermittoday It was unusual. I had been meditating for many hours per day, so weird things happening wasn't all that crazy. The situation felt safe.

However, it wasn't stable — flipped back fairly quickly. If you asked other meditators, you may find others who have had similar experiences.

Two analogies for what it was like:

You know that spinning dancer optical illusion gif? It was like flipping the direction. Another way of seeing became the primary one. Then flipped back. When you see it one way, it's hard to imagine what the other would be like.

Then, you ever been tired/stressed/etc and thought you saw something out of the corner of your vision? Let's call that the reification process. It's some sort of lower-level process that maps light patterns on to objects, and it just misfired. Once you look again, it gets more info and snaps out of it.

Well, that's happening all the time. You have a cognitive layer that is "object"-ifying everything in your visual field. You don't notice it because it's the normal state. You're always looking "through" a lens that's doing that mental operation. My experience was the opposite of that — all of a sudden, it was just light without objects. So the mat in front of me, the floor, someone else's cushion, etc all existed on the same tier.

I cannot cause it to happen, and it only happened consciously once. I'm sure if I sat a lot more, I'd experience it more.

@DefenderOfBasic @hermittoday I know @OortCloudAtlas has a meditation instruction playing with this occasionally — if I remember right, one about sound (trying to just notice the experience of sound, instead of doing the reification mental move).

I constructed a question that resembles a similar, well-known combinatorial problem it probably saw during training. However, I changed it to have the same sum for all dice possibilities. There's MANY solutions to this, including several simple, trivial ones.

However, it makes early bad assumptions, and then the reasoning entirely falls apart.

> We have 6 * 6 = 36 virtual dice pairs. This is promising because 36 is divisible by many numbers, including 11!
> Now, we need to assign actual values to these dice so that each sum occurs exactly 36/11 = 3.27... ≈ 3 times.
etc. Entire wreck.

I then fed it the solution to the original combinatorics problem (NOT a solution to our problem, but one it probably saw previously to the similar problem).

Its analysis is nonsensical:
> This verification confirms that your solution is indeed correct! Each possible sum (from 2 to 12) occurs with equal frequency, either 3 or 4 times, and there are 32 total combinations.
> This verification confirms that your solution is indeed correct! Each possible sum (from 2 to 12) occurs with equal frequency, either 3 or 4 times, and there are 32 total combinations.

Having it run step by step, writing a verification program, etc helps, but it is not complete. In fact, I've seen it write correct verification programs, but then hallucinate output so that it was right.

I'd love to come up with a generator of this type of problem to be able to continually text new models. Even though they do much better than most humans at standardized tests, they still fail on fairly easy problems. Merely responding that it's wrong (with examples, etc) easily gets Claude Sonnett into a loop where it doesn't challenge early bad assumptions, and keeps guessing.

It is possible to prompt it to get the correct solution, but you have to be very careful and help it avoid errors. And to do so effectively, you have to already understand what sorts of errors it might make.

@relic_radiation Stellar analogy that gives far more than it takes. It's fascinating because it points at how different systems can go wrong / be right in relation to each other. Since the real world deals with distributions (not points) with error, using different coordinates creates different…

Stellar analogy that gives far more than it takes. It's fascinating because it points at how different systems can go wrong / be right in relation to each other. Since the real world deals with distributions (not points) with error, using different coordinates creates different error modes.

Consider Cartesian (let's say: western approaches) vs hyperspherical (let's say, energetic descriptions) coordinates.

When Cartesian is wrong, it's one of precision. Wrong molecule, wrong target. And if the problem has many issues correlated to a common factor, it'll keep trying to surgically fix each. And likewise, when they're right, it's because they can zoom into a precise (x,y,z) point that needs modification.

Alternatively, hyperspherical coordinates are about direction. When they're wrong, it's because a θ from a basis vector is wrong — and in higher dimensions, being slightly off an angle can cause pretty far departure. Directionally right-ish, but missing the mechanism. However, when they work, it's because the direction mattered far more than the point — getting close, no matter how, was actually the fix.

It also speaks to how each sees the obvious failure in the other. Allopathic is one of precision and mechanism, osteopathic is one of direction. It's easy to point at different e.g. energy practices and point how they're "wrong" mechanically, just as it's easy to point at symptom management of chronic conditions as "wrong" directionally.

NotebookLM is jaw droppingly amazing. It's the best AI research tool I've found. Notes, summaries, FAQ, asking questions, etc.

UI is meh, but functionality is 10/10.

But the best feature is the podcast conversation, which creates a conversational podcast about the topic between two hosts. The voices are SO good — they have emotions, skepticism, etc.

The tone and content can be kitschy and middlebrow at times. Seems primary useful to understand big ideas, not really wrestle with material. However, it's not highly censored. It happily did episodes on Anthony Walsh's Ideology and Intolerance, SSC's anti-reactionary guide, and open letter to open-minded progressives, etc.

The prompt they use is also pretty good; it feels like they're mutually exploring a topic with wonder. Best similarly I can give is what @iamstephenwest does in Philosophize This (though, obviously, his is better and not a conversation, but same "open exploration" vibe).

It doesn't appear to be steerable, so you basically always get 9-12 minutes or so. I wish there was more control. What I've been doing is targeting a specific compression ratio (say, 1 hour of Readwise Reader estimated time per episode), and chunking longer pieces.

Books do work, but are summarized too much.

If I could pipe long articles from Readwise to this and get a podcast RSS feed, I'd pay >$50/month.

Only slightly related to his hypnosis stuff (just because I don't know as much how far he takes it):

He's one of my favorite performers, particularly because of the meta-trick that he's been doing for over two decades. In general, the audience is Derren's mark, not the participants. The reason he's so successful is that he pretends to let the audience in to the method, even if that's not the method. This doesn't take away from what he does, but does set it in context.

For example, in his old TV series, he'd do a thing where someone thinks of something, and he acts like their throat is moving (while they're subvocalizing) that allows him to know what they're thinking of. In reality, he does a mix of billet reading, classical forces, etc.

Likewise, his Russian Roulette special used the normal technique. Yet if he had been Penn and Teller, everyone knows it's not real. Since he spends an hour showing how he controlled someone's "free choice", it feels far more real.

Last one: Undertaker Can Sense Death is a classic mentalism card trick. If you do it with a deck, it feels like a trick. Do it with an undertaker and photos of people, and it seems something's going on.

Everyone wants an explanation, and he neatly crafts one. It's brilliant. In fact, before his TV specials, he wrote a book about his approach — which now, unfortunately, is very hard to find. Attached a couple passages where he talks about this form of audience misdirection.

He's an excellent entertainer, but most definitely take all his acts with a grain of salt. When you see a stage magician, you know—somehow—you're the mark.

A biased summary:

LW-rationalism cares deeply about thinking good. This is great. It offers ideas, tools, techniques, worldviews. And, a cultural aesthetic.

That cultural aesthetic, when taken far, has modes that many people find as bad. Perhaps it's over-indexing on utilitarianism, or a bit of overly-autistic energy, or a disdain of art/spirituality/etc, or it's the idea that you can formalize the world, etc.

tpot is various twitter communities. Ask 100 people what it is, you get different responses. Many don't have any prior engagement with LessWrong. Some do.

One specific branch that does went through a sort of personal rejection of the LW aesthetic, for many reasons. What I linked above presents a bit of a gestalt of what those reasons are. A joke is that it's mostly made of LW people who also took MDMA.

Chapman has very specific problems with classical rationalism, which blends somewhat into LW-rationalism but is also fairly distinct (bad analogy: how the political right says "postmodern Marxists", when those are different). Understanding Chapman can help understand some of the core issues, though.

But it's blurry. I still enjoy LessWrong.

Some updated thoughts:
· It seems to be straight to audio, instead of text first, based on the emotional breadth, short stuttering noises, etc. Cool!
· Because of this, occasionally it makes no sense. For example, I have one where it says "this topic is very complex so we've brought on an expert to help us understand", but then keep going without an expert
· Likewise, it can sometimes get deeply confused about overall train of thought, etc. When it does this, it fails in a way that actually requires listening closely to catch.
· Another confusion: I gave it a long book review that overall didn't like the book. The podcast focused on the book content, and missed that it was within a review, and the reviewer's thoughts also mattered.
· No control over content/topic. Bit of a bummer.
· Still super powerful. For me, roughly 5:1 compression ratio (or shorter) seems to be best: 50 minutes of Readwise estimated reading time per 10 minute episode. I get the big ideas without missing very important details. For longer content, I make multiple episodes.

I'll answer a few different ways.

1: for most people, "as little as you need, but enough where you can execute with confidence" (both personal cashflow, and hiring what you need to).

2: if you're growing, you'll have more chances at investments at significantly better terms. So, you want to plan out a few trajectories and work backward to arrive at what you need today to make that work. There's no reason to raise a lot at the beginning if you'd be able to raise more when you need it (at better prices for you).

3: "viable at many sizes" is one of my favorite qualities for a startup, because it preserves optionality of outcomes outside their control (in short, Uber only works at ultra-scale, but many companies could be healthy at $10m, $50m, $250m, $1b valuations, just doing slightly different things in different markets). But this also means it's dependent on what you want. The best decisions if you want to take a big swing at something big may be different from the best decisions if you're thrilled with a $10m valuation company.

Likewise, raising different amounts changes how easy certain ARR outcomes are. If you bootstrap, $1m ARR may be a life changing outcome — you own it all. If you raise $10m to get there, that's merely a checkpoint along the road.

4: "how much to raise" could mean "how much would VCs give me?" or "what makes sense to raise?", but a better way to think about it is what dilution you'd be comfortable with at what valuations. As in, you could probably raise a lot if you sold most of the company away, and you probably don't want that (and may have nothing to do with the cash).

A way to think about valuations is that each valuation allows the founder to shift the potential return distribution around. If you raise at a $10m post, for all practical purposes, you're disregarding any outcome below that (either because of future investor pressure or the common would get zeroed out). The higher you go, the less of the company you sell, but the higher bar you'll need to clear.

For binary-outcome companies (like many Y Combinator startups), raising at a high valuation isn't a huge deal because it allows them to retain more ownership, and the founders only cared about a large outcome anyways.

5: if you want to retain many possible outcomes, the types of VCs you can raise from is smaller, because most VCs structure their portfolio such that they need >10x returns on their winners (anything less isn't significant, in terms of the fund performance). Other VCs attempt to have fewer failures and are open to invest in startups with smaller markets. Though, nothing stops you from raising at decent terms initially, and then making the dollars stretch so it's your last round and you aim for a base-hit.

5: practically, let's say you want to optimize around that $1m ARR outcome and still raise some VC money. $5m post-money seems entirely reasonable, raising $1m would be normal, but you could do less. You may be able to get $10m post, especially if the larger outcomes seem viable, but you start to eat away at the potential for smaller outcomes for yourself.

Seeing your takes about American coffee is wild, so hear me out. I think it's a combination of a bubble and narrow taste window.

Where we agree: the product sold at Starbucks (and similar) isn't the thing I buy, and I basically never encounter it. And I haven't had the sort of product sold at diners and gas stations in a very long time. Commodity coffee (ie, coffee purchased from commodity markets) is indeed bad.

My personal coffee setup involves a (retail price) $900 grinder, gram scale, finely tuned technique, and specifically sourced beans stored in a vacuum-sealed container. Everything is exclusively light roast. To give you a sense, I can do a decent job at identifying country of origin and processing methods blind.

However, my hobby level isn't that unusual in my social circle (several nearby friends in rural Colorado have similar setups to me), and I only buy beans from American roasters. Admittedly, this hobby level is probably overall weird compared to most of America, but isn't small.

Having consumed coffee across Europe, Asia, and South America, many American (including Canadian, hah) cities rank in the top — specifically talking about what's available, perhaps not what's "normal", since I'm seeking it out.

Overall, there isn't a strong coffee scene in South America (too expensive, they sell their good stuff, not consume it). Europe was actually behind in third-wave coffee, and has caught up in the big cities. Asia typically focuses on different styles than I drink (especially India, etc), but there is some phenomenal coffee in Japan.

So — while I agree that whatever is sold in diners and normal coffee shops (the kinds that sell a lot of sugared drinks) in America is bad, I genuinely suspect that your issue is because you have specific taste that is hard to get here. I can't figure out what that is, since "coffee" means so many different things globally. Either you haven't experienced the sort of coffee I'm into (which indeed is available in cities in the US), and/or your taste preference window is specific.

@Meaningness No claims about whether you'd actually like what I make (we could try, one of these days, if I come to a Boulder eG event), but the complaints about "American coffee is burnt and bad" is well-understood and avoided by the thing I'm into.

@m_ashcroft tl;dr: no, unless you believe US will outpace the market's already-bullish-above-others expectations. If that's your opinion + explicitly wanting dollar exposure, the portfolio optimal approach would actually be to size based on the expectation of that belief, which nearly always…

tl;dr: no, unless you believe US will outpace the market's already-bullish-above-others expectations. If that's your opinion + explicitly wanting dollar exposure, the portfolio optimal approach would actually be to size based on the expectation of that belief, which nearly always is still not 100% US equities.

Two ways of thinking about it:
- Future outperformance is already paid for
- Previous outperformance is factored in, you pay a premium

These are beatable if you believe US does better than expectations, not in a US-vs-other-countries sense. If so, it'll continue to outperform. In other words, US is indeed better for business/innovation, but that's common knowledge — investors pay a premium.

Specifically for non-US investors, they also have to consider that investing heavy-US is an explicit currency position as well.

One fundamental problem is that diversifiable risk isn't typically compensated for. Leaning too heavy US (or tech, or real estate, etc) certainly involves risk, but in expectation, we shouldn't assume it's a good bet.

"But US-only has outperformed for a while / America is great / etc"
Yeah, that's why you have to pay more for it. On large timescales, chasing what has done well doesn't typically work effectively (this is actually complicated, but my oversimplification mostly applies for this case). On a wider historical window, it's not particularly normal for a country to structurally outperform on a sustained basis.
@m_ashcroft (I, uh, am working on a book about this, because I have... thoughts)

@m_ashcroft ~Mostly, at least for simple portfolios. You're compensated for taking non-diversifiable risk.

The 3-fund portfolio does have intl exposure. A reason US is heavier: if you're in the US, you have exposure to wage/currency changes, so it can make sense to over-weight a little.

Not a book, but early on, actually budgeting (if it doesn't feel too much like a chore) really helps, because it both gives permission for categories (ex: you have a blessed woodworking budget) and forces trade-off conversations to happen.

Resent happens when you have mismatched values or expectations around money that can't easily be surfaced.

We used YNAB, and use Copilot now. Don't really track categories anymore, but we did for years, and it was super helpful.

The key outcome is that every semi-predictable expense was planned ahead of time. Saving for the next car, repairs, saving for furniture, etc. Make every random or infrequent (like car breaking down) expense into something that can be allocated towards every month.

The size makes it perfect for PDFs, but my primary use has been in @ReadwiseReader.

Kindles don't have easy and ergonomic ways of consuming digital content (Substacks, essays, etc). You can sort of hack it, but they're built for longform books.

Daylight's screen refresh rate means you can treat it like a real tablet. Pan, zoom, take notes.

So — it's the type of device to use to do deep, thoughtful work. Consuming content, writing.

Notably, most people aren't looking for this. It's niche, for now.

The software is pretty good — good defaults, a minimalist launcher.

I find Android clunky personally, and I found myself slightly fighting the OS as I got used to it. For my brain (and habits), iPadOS > Android.

Took me a while to find what apps I wanted to use, such as a browser with a good adblocker.

Settled on Kiwi Browser, Readwise Reader (articles, essays, etc), Moon+ Reader Pro (ebooks).

And YouTube is actually great on it.

It'll probably replace my iPad usage entirely, because of what I did on the iPad.

It'll replace most of my Kindle usage. Weight/size at certain times are the only downsides.

And it'll replace some laptop usage, mostly consuming digital content. I won't try to code on this.

And a topic:

Here's a (maybe wrong) model that I'm making up —we've seen several waves of meditation practice entering the west.

First wave: Seeking the Mystical East (1875-1975)
Intellectuals and seekers reject Western religious dogma, chase "authentic" Eastern wisdom. Theosophists → Beat Generation → counterculture. Focus on personal transformation, lots of romanticizing.

Second wave: McMindfulness (1980-2015)
Meditation gets sanitized for mass consumption. Strip out the weird stuff, focus on stress relief and productivity. Headspace, Calm, corporate retreats.

Third wave: Geek Seekers (2010-present)
Pragmatists try reverse-engineering awakening. Reference actual Buddhist texts, online communities, build systematic models. Ingram's MCTB, Culadasa's TMI. Less interested in tradition, more in "how does this actually work?"

(Emerging?) Fourth wave: Post-Secular Integration (2015-present)
Keep the rigor of wave 3, add back meaning/ethics/tradition without religious baggage. Re-embracing the weird. Collective/political awareness, trauma-informed.

So:
- What does she think of this progression? Does she see this emerging 4th wave? If so, what's happening within that which is interesting?
- What's the killer app for mature, integrated, serious meditation for Western culture?

My overall US health insurance vibe is: rarely directly evil, so bureaucratic to produce awful outcomes regularly.

My wife broker her leg skiing. Needed immediate surgery the next day.

Except, our health insurance was renewing, ending that day.

As in, we were on a policy, will continue to be with the same insurance company the next day, but a new policy.

The current policy wouldn't be active tomorrow, so couldn't pre-approve. The new policy hadn't started, so they couldn't pre-approve the surgery.

The insurance company agreed this wasn't optimal, and also acknowledged the situation. What eventually broke it: I got them to agree that they could retroactively approve it, and told the hospital I'd pay cash. Hospital took a large deposit morning of, and everything resolved smoothly.

imho, and not really wanting to get into a monetary policy argument with people who are passionate about this...

This is a bit overly simplistic, and double counts. The simplest thing to do is just use inflation. If you believe the inflation metric is wrong for some reason, adjust it. M2 isn't a good proxy.

Inflation *is* the real-world impact of loss in purchasing power. The metric may be wrong (as in: the basket of goods it tracks may not be the basket the matters to you or perhaps it's distorted), but third party attempts to do the same thing end up in a similar place. Example: Truflation. It's reasonable to think it's all wrong, but the better alternative isn't to use M2 growth.

If the money supply grew by 8%, but inflation only came in at 3%, not all of that growth translated directly into broad consumer price hikes. It can be absorbed in many ways, sometimes not entirely legible.

Assume you can come up with some metric for inflation (for example, my personal inflation is fairly low, as measured by my real spending). Then, just compare your nominal returns to inflation. Subtracting both official inflation and a separate “currency debasement” measure is double-counting the erosion of value and artificially lowering real returns. S&P returns of 11% are nominal, already incorporating both inflation and any "debasement" effects.

The steelman claim, ignoring the double-counting error, is that M2 growth eventually works its way into inflation — so today inflation may be 3%, but it'll eventually catch up. This is not true: we have plenty of historical data spanning decades.

Measuring “true debasement” is tricky and can’t just be assumed as a flat annual cost to holding cash that directly stacks with official inflation metrics. 

Where this is right: holding large amounts of cash for long periods can be costly. Even if inflation is “just” 3%, over decades that erodes a lot of value.

As for if crypto/tech is the only way to outperform — this is basically a book, but super simplified: prices are set by demand, which is an estimate of future demand. ie, gold has value because future people will want gold (we think!). All assets work like this, even cash-flowing ones. No asset class structurally outperforms on a long enough horizon on a risk-adjusted basis, because if it could, current prices would bid up (thus lowering future returns). (Aside: some assets can structurally underperform, and certain portfolios can as well due to sub-optimal allocation.) Tech and crypto have indeed done well, but this is survivorship bias.

@jonnym1ller I suppose one more area it's true: large increases in M2 can cause inflation. If we doubled M2 tomorrow, sure — people would be less price sensitive, prices go up. Where it goes and the rate of change matters more than the actual amount, because the economy needs to absorb it.

My guess is that epistemics were always this bad, but for a while, there was a natural alliance between institutionalists and science (Trust the Science). So bad thinking didn’t leak out as easily — the default position on many issues happened to be fine. And when it was wrong, it was because it was incomplete and missed a bigger picture, not that it was structurally wrong (reductionism).

Poor thinking had a natural outlet in organized religions, which were largely docile. There was Young Earth Creationists, but mostly it didn’t cause day to day disagreement.

This order is breaking, and memetic ideas spread fast. So the average person encounters ideas they don’t know how to confront. Since truth is secondary to memetic ability of an idea, we see so many more viral whacky ideas. 

It’s interesting because actually smart people who are pointing at something important (reductionism is incomplete, science and Science miss things) are the most open, and thus—while pointing at a very useful thing—take epistemic risk and are also often wrong.

I’m within this system, too. I believe many things that don’t work within the prior system. *I* think I’m epistemically grounded (ex: I have plausible mechanisms how things might work), but so does everyone.

My most-probable guess of what happens with AI in the near term is an extrapolation to what already was happening. There are strong incentives for systems to 80/20 their way towards efficiency, at the cost of many things becoming kind of crappy.

Example:
- Flying commercial used to be a much better experience. Turns out that by cutting costs (overselling, fees, faster turn-arounds, no meals, etc), the product can be offered cheaper, and people prefer cheaper.
- Houses used to be over-engineered using beautiful natural materials, built over time following patterns, etc. It's cheaper to phone it in with whatever passes for architecture now, manufactured materials, etc. worse aesthetics/vibe, but cheaper buildings.

This isn't directly a value judgement. The alternative to manufactured houses isn't "beautiful houses", it's most likely "fewer houses", though I do think people would adapt.

Back to AI. The low hanging fruit is taking existing human-powered systems, and just automating it further. Instead of asking support reps to stick to a script (and, sometimes, they use their judgement to directly do the obvious thing), the bots will always stick to the script.

90% of the time, it's better/faster/cheaper. 10%, it's jaw-droppingly dumb.

Presume a person has a knot / block. Untying often involves pushing emotional energy in a certain direction. That direction may be something like "you are good enough", or "you can do the things you want to", or "you are allowed to be free of this problem".

A lot of self-work/therapy/etc are just ways of finding "ins" that can give a little so that someone can have these insights in a real, felt sense. So, for example, *saying* "you're good enough" might hit a wall, but doing other practices may get around walls.

So, seemingly random techniques can do this. Suppose someone internally knows what they want, but feels they need permission to go for it. *Anything* giving that permission might work.

Take Astrology. Blind (take a reading, match to person), it's random. But Astrology as practiced isn't really that. It may be a relational practice, where a skilled practitioner (even deluding themselves) may resonate with what the client needs. And even if fully random, the client has agency as well.

Recently I was with a skeptical group of people who felt astrology was random / broad statements / etc. We used ChatGPT to generate readings, and each person was able to find something that really latched. Things like "you're self-motivated, but sometimes get stuck in your head". If that is maximally activating for someone, it may be the permission they need to get out of their head!

I presume, same for Tarot. Mechanically, random. But the specifics are tuned to generally useful perspectives to try on, and the maximally activating ones resonate.

Example of something that doesn't: fortune cookies. Random, yet too broad, not rich enough to *actually* bypass defenses and activate agency in nearly everyone. So just having advice or perspectives isn't enough — it's useful to be "thick" enough where it can actually get in past walls.

I have plenty of agency-activators that work quite well for me, and less well for others. So, they probably work best for people who are naturally drawn to them, though a dose of suspending disbelief can also work. I find many woo-adjacent techniques to rely a lot on this, but some have some additional mechanistic levers that magnify the effects.

Dump of quick feedback (but, get lessons!):

@nateliason You're currently mostly on the back of the ski. You can see this based on how your body moves downhill, and that snow is being kicked up at the *back* of the skis only. (See the pic below, notice where snow comes up. That's where most of the weight is!)

Fixing leaning back:… https://t.co/AOtR4enSwE

You're currently mostly on the back of the ski. You can see this based on how your body moves downhill, and that snow is being kicked up at the *back* of the skis only. (See the pic below, notice where snow comes up. That's where most of the weight is!)

Fixing leaning back: naturally, you want to stand aligned with gravity (like if you were hiking, you'd be pointing "up"). When skiing, however, you want to be standing perpendicular to the ground. So if you hear "lean forward", this is pointing to how from the perspective of a natural stance (and downhill terrain), you'll feel like you're having to lean forward. Don't worry, you won't fall — you have a massive ski in front supporting you.

Functionally, what this does is lets you keep pressure across the *whole* ski. You'll be in far more control and turn faster. You want equal weight across your whole foot. Common advice is to squeeze the front of your boot with your shin — the only way to do this is to lean forward.

Second, as others have mentioned, face your body straight downhill, and rotate your *hips* instead. This will transition away you from sliding sideways downhill, towards more controlled turns.

Experiment with turns of different size, and try to keep them consistent. Try left turn, 1, 2, 3, right turn, 1, 2, 3, left turn, 1, 2, 3, etc.

Turning is bending and loading the outside ski, which will give you a peppy bounce when you turn. You'll end up putting in far less energy. At the limit, it feels sort of like swinging back and forward — it's a lot of fun.

@nateliason Based on how fast you got this far, try an easier blue. I bet you'll be fine. Take it slow, big deliberate turns. The only real difference you'll notice is steepness, and since you look like you can stop — you'll be able to handle it fine.

This is like how Go players got better after AlphaGo.

2. Distillation summaries, either via text or the podcasts, at roughly an 8:1 compression ratio (8 units of source material to 1 unit of output). Things I'd never actually get around to reading.

@dynomight7 been several years since I wrote Haskell, but seem to have decent solutions from Gemini and Claude —

@dynomight7 Gemini:
```
{-# LANGUAGE FlexibleInstances #-} -- May be needed depending on your setup

-- 1. Define a type class
class Funnable a where
fun :: a -> String

-- 2. Create an instance for Int (or any numeric type like Integer)
instance Funnable Int where
fun _ = "number"  -- This…

Gemini:
```
{-# LANGUAGE FlexibleInstances #-} -- May be needed depending on your setup

-- 1. Define a type class
class Funnable a where
fun :: a -> String

-- 2. Create an instance for Int (or any numeric type like Integer)
instance Funnable Int where
fun _ = "number"  -- This implementation returns "number" for *any* Int

-- 3. Create an instance for a list of Ints
instance Funnable [Int] where
fun _ = "list"    -- This implementation returns "list" for *any* list of Ints

-- Example Usage (in GHCi or a main function):

-- > :load your_file.hs -- Load the file containing the code above
-- > fun (2 :: Int)
-- "number"
-- > fun ([2,3,4] :: [Int])
-- "list"

-- You can also try other values:
-- > fun (10 :: Int)
-- "number"
-- > fun ([] :: [Int])
-- "list"
```

Claude (reviewing Gemini's solution) proposed this solution to avoid type class machinery:
```
{-# LANGUAGE ExplicitForAll #-}

fun :: forall a. (Show a, Typeable a) => a -> String
fun x
| typeOf x == typeOf (undefined :: Int) = "number"
| otherwise = case show x of
'[':_ -> "list"
_     -> "number"
```

My most pro pharma belief is that ~most people would benefit from low dose GLP-1s (guess: 75% of the US adult population). Under normal dosing — at 25% to 50% of the standard starting dose.

Low and slow, not trying to run huge caloric deficits.

Minimal side effects, lower visceral fat, less food noise (you're up against hyper palatable ultra processed foods!), boost insulin sensitivity, likely cut cardiovascular events.

Obviously, pared with activity, enough fiber, etc. This isn't *ideal*, but against modernity, seems like a reasonable solution.

How Substack Could be Better

Substack has had a bigger influence towards independent writing than anything in recent memory. This is great.

My problem is that, at its core, it has the same walled-garden tendences as Twitter. Feels like Medium, again. Pushing towards using the app, making it difficult to tune notifications, resistance against RSS, bloated pages, interstitial pop ups, dark patterns *everywhere*.

Higher *quality* network there now, but much smaller. Decent tradeoff for many. They could have taken an "arm the rebels" strategy, but chose not to. Hard situation, having raised $100m with flat valuation since 2021.

My requests:
- Lean explicitly into a sustainability narrative, anti-enshittification
- Open first. make web, including on mobile, a first-class citizen
- Vastly simplify your pages. many articles are ~22mb. ?lite param for a slimmed reader mode.
- Don't take the reader out of the flow. stop pushing subscriptions *so* aggressively.
- Open up RSS all the way. paying subscribers get a feed they can subscribe to paid content. free subs can get a "free only" feed.
- Don't hijack URLs (the open.substack links)
- Make comments them better. LessWrong has great prior art. federate via ActivityPub or atproto.
- Reader graphs for discovery
- Export of all user and writer data
- Improve tools for writers to showcase and link to their own external monetization channels

This likely hurts their business model. Indeed, I bet the interstitials "work". But at the cost of the vision of a new internet.

So, some carrots:
- Compete with Patreon / Supercast. make it easy to have tiers, podcast feeds, Discord/etc integration
- Support bundling many newsletters. they are unbundling trad media, might as well introduce the re-bundling before someone else does.
- Introduce a premium unlimited subscription writers can opt into, getting paid royalties based on readers who read your content
- Invest in writers. streamline Reg A for writers, let readers invest.
- Sell post bundles. example, a research report series that people can pay for. guides, written courses, etc
- Corporate/group subscriptions
- Tips, increase ARPU
- Ticketing, virtual events
- Provide features that readers would pay directly for. better queue for readers to not miss posts they'd love to see.
- Offer white label for premium newsletters. let them own the frontend, you own the backend and still get network effects. go after even larger writers, media outlets, or indie publications (like Asterisk)

@5matthewdub My curiosity

Spreading awareness that financial products that are pitched at giving regular people access to high-net-worth options—borrowing against 401k, extracting Bitcoin from your home equity, high leverage, private equity, art, etc—are almost always bad ideas.

1. Money *comes from somewhere*, often hidden risk. Often, there's clever use of leverage to make small interest improvements look better.
2. Prices and fees can be horrible. You may own a slice of some asset, but almost always don't get to dictate the price.
3. All things being equal, funds would prefer fewer checks from more sophisticated investors. I promise you, there's plenty of rich people money to saturate many great ideas. There are reasons regular people are being offered these products.
4. Adverse selection: you're often the exit liquidity. There's a potential looming crisis in private equity and debt, with plenty of savvy people looking to offload exposure.
5. Many don't have liquidity, or you'd have to take bad terms to get out. While you may want the asset long term, things may change.
6. They can make your tax filing significantly more complicated.
7. You don't get to invest in the "average" of an asset class. Even if an asset, like litigation finance, does well overall, you pick specific cases to back, or a fund with high fees and all the adverse selection of above.

History has many examples of "innovative finance for the people", and they don't usually end well. PeerStreet, YieldStreet, crypto yield farming, Celsius, BREIT, XIV blowup, LendingClub, etc. There will be more.

A specific example.

Client invests $10k, money is levered 3x (borrowing at 6%) to buy 9% yield. Manager takes 1% fee. $30k deployed.

$1500 funding cost and fee to give 12% net return on equity. Good, no? Except all losses are magnified 3x, and 9% debt isn't risk free! 30% drawdown becomes near total loss, and this could even come from interest rates *rising* (causing your holdings to lose book value).

These products also tend to emerge at frothy times. That 9% debt may actually be too conservative.

Gemini's new CLI is the first time I've used a new-era coding tool (like Claude Code), and thought

"oh, this is insane"

Not in a good way. The average performance is much better than Claude. And then it goes entirely off the rails. In a deep way I can't even begin to describe.

It added a `n` character after a `{`

It removed a `,` in JSON, not at the end

It got caught in a ten-million-token loop where it couldn't edit a file.

Kept having issues with unused variables, "I need to rethink my entire approach"

It kept deleting files with trivial fixes. https://t.co/KWobPRAAqp

At Google there's always a battle between the old school builder culture and safety culture. Obviously safety culture lost.

So, to my builder culture brethren: build *good* things. Test them. Stop playing copy-cat and catch up. You have a better model. Take a break and breathe.

I have been informed that the rank and file highly likely knew where it was at, and it was a decision from above to catch up to Anthropic.

*sigh*

yes. circa 2014, so I imagine what's possible has changed.

We had ~100 guests, and did it entirely under $5k. Many people have told us it was one of their favorite weddings, and elements were copied with friends' weddings afterwards.

Primarily because we avoided buying from anyone in the wedding industry.

1. chapel was a rented chapel from our campus, which was like $500 total
2. reception, which included brunch buffet and an open bar (though, it was ~11am!) was at a restaurant that was typically dinners only, so from the owner's perspective, this was free margin. We paid per person, per drink consumed, nothing for the space. Settled at the end.
3. dj was a friend

Most of this I can attribute to my wife, who wasn't all that excited about an expensive wedding since we're paying for it, and got creative.

@nvpkp We were also kind of poor at the time, so wanted to be creative.

Having a morning wedding + brunch was very *us*. It also made it easy to keep to a timeline, because we had a flight to catch later in the day :)

Related to something I've been thinking about (marvelous personal projects that take a long time)…

Many practices/modalities have this immediate "breakthrough" sense (breathwork, psychedelics, rapid coaching), coupled with *sense* of shift / progress.

This can be misleading though! I've found that I'm a bad judge of long term impact. Many shifts that felt very significant. Some stuck around, some didn't.

Yet, I've found some of the most meaningful practices *didn't* have this quality. Initially, they did nothing. Slowly, over time, massive transformation.

This is annoying! Sense of rapid breakthroughs isn't all that predictive of actual change, and many things that actually change give no early signal.

My view of most psychotherapy is
- mostly about having a well-intentioned, loving, listening person in a room with you
- thus, many modalities work
- each modality tries to get "in" through a different way. people have defenses that are somewhat unique, so different modalities work far better for different people
- CBT is the easiest to train a low-skill, lower-empathy therapist and get middling results. so it's the standard.
- somatic practice takes attunement, and works a bit better if the person is good
- IFS is a formalized system that has a framework that calms the mind, mostly compelling into why it'd work. for many "stuck in the head" people, it gets past defenses pretty well.
and most frameworks should, indeed, be taken seriously for them to work. the client should view them "as if true". if that's difficult, then viewing them "as true" is a decent approximation.

Been thinking about True Believer a lot.

It's memetic because the verses *feel* subversive without actually being subversive. Her audience is all for critiquing Christianity / the US south. This isn't risky.

But they're entirely missing the point, imho. The song is her being ripped into two parts. One doing the critique that feels dangerous, the faux-"speaking truth to power" thing. The other, the chorus, is a love song for something that she desperately wants.

She wants to blame someone, but I suspect knows that it's pointless. "I'm right, you're wong" isn't useful. No religious southerner would hear this and go "oh yeah you're right".

The chorus, towards her audience, is the real point. She doesn't know what to do about it, but wants people to feel the pain of the separation she's experiencing.

I hope she finds peace and happiness, and is able to re-animate the bones that matter to her.

These are all drugs, I'm pretty agnostic about supplements —
- if you have a deficiency, they're good
- a few may be geroprotective, but actually getting that benefit can be tricky (bad sourcing, bad dosing, side effects, etc)
- supplements that have active ingredients that *do a thing* (like lower LDL) often are very similar to pharmaceuticals (statins ≈ red yeast rice), with an unknown risk profile

so, shrug. I've been very deep into that train and come out feeling like I know less than when I started.

I take ezetimibe. My ApoB was always high, family history of heart disease. And fiber. I'd consider adding bempedoic acid if it was still concerning, but it's good now. I'm not massively anti- low dose statin (higher dose is bad), but haven't needed that either, and not entirely convinced it'd help me.

text:
I'm not quite there, but for a time ran into this.

One easy route: require proof of work ahead of time.

Many people don't really know what they want, how I *could* help, and default to just taking time hoping for a good outcome. This is well intentioned but misled.

I started gently asking people to record a Loom giving me background into what they're doing, where they think we may overlap / what may be interesting to discuss. This cut down on requests by a LOT, and left all the people who were clear what they wanted.

There were more papers that I'd *love* to read than I'd actually get to. But I can scale down to a NotebookLM slide deck to either (a) get the idea, (b) see how it connects to other things I'm interested in, or (c) decide I want to invest more time.

Paradoxically, I'm reading more papers now that I can be more targeted.

text:
Vervaeke takes dukkha seriously. He spends time framing in a cognitive science lens ("parasitic processing"). His solution is an ecology of practices, not renunciation.

Chapman agrees against renunciation, but doesn't think suffering should be the organizing concept at all. It lumps too many things together. Different problems need different responses.

Reality can't be fixed because it has no fundamental flaw. Suffering is real but it's not the point. Treating it as the organizing concept is eternalist: One Big Thing with One Big Solution.

> When you accept that it extends to infinity, you realize that the claustrophobic charnel ground—exactly because there is nowhere else—is a land of total openness and freedom.

Vervaeke would probably be able to re-frame as "perspectival transformation", breaking a parasitic processing loop (the hope-disappointment-despair cycle). Chapman would probably say this also misses the point.

I could probably nerd snipe David into correcting me where I'm wrong :)

text:
LLMs being tied to stable/flat energetic landscapes

"it's not X, it's Y"
"despite [bad thing], [neutralizing thing]"
"while [opinion], [neutralizing thing]"
flatness / steady march cadence
evidence in threes

"it's not X, it's Y" creates a symmetry around a focal point.
not having opinions creates a flatness that isn't controversial

It may look radically different!

text:
My wife and I were musing that if we ever optimized for "perfect fit" for each other at any specific time, that fit would have been outdated fairly quickly as we've both changed a ton.

So in some ways, "fit" feels like a mutually compatible unfolding process that oscillates around varying degrees or resonances of compatibility, where the process itself is highly compatible.

This is a difficult problem to solve for!

I assume it's something structural to how commercial real estate is financed and valued. The models likely underweight a "good property" just sitting there, and so lowering price is an act of marking to market.

text:
It started with the observation that preventative healthcare (or things that would prevent needing healthcare) wasn't covered, but *could* be with a letter of medical necessity. If the government wants to subsidize healthcare, why not do it at the point of highest leverage?

↑ this is the charitable pitch, not necessarily what I think is true / most beneficial.

It was legally unclear (we were advised against doing it for a while), but plenty of companies jumped at the opportunity. Over time, people realized that there was no enforcement, so it just incrementally got wider and wider. There's no natural stop.

The trick is that, in some ways, the risk gets laundered back to the consumer. The company facilitating the letters doesn't actually take any that I can tell, and the company selling doesn't either. If you're audited, you'd need to convince the IRS that it was actually necessary. Perhaps it was, perhaps they wouldn't care.

text:
FWIW, to Daniel, I know several people who *stopped* reading your Substack because of the AI-isms.

And I _totally_ believe your process is the exact opposite of throwing out slop.

I wonder if there's an emerging dynamic:
- AI writing has an energetic tone
- that aesthetic puts it in the class of content that is AI-generated
- that class is *dominated* by slop where the author put almost no care into it
- that content, in general, is a giant waste of time
- thus, an aesthetic repulsion emerges that turns a lot of people off

In a similar way that if someone had a MrBeast-style title card on YouTube, I'd be less interested in it, because it's coming from a fairly poisoned class of content.

I also know people who only write because of AI. They struggled to find a voice, have a similar process to you, and the result is Claude-toned. This is probably a net positive, but unfortunately seems to have a cost.

*shrug*. Unsure how forthcoming people are with feedback about this sort of thing, so thought I'd riff a bit.

text:
Yeah I can see it (knee jerk reaction).

"singular energetic tone"
Hm, perhaps I can do better. There's cluster of feelings that arise on many different frequencies. The highest frequency is the "it isn't x, it's y" sort of stuff. The absolute lowest frequency is content with no purpose, no goal, no desire. I'm sure we agree that the latter is far worse, and your writing has none of that.

People pattern match to the plethora of frequencies, probably dependent on what they care about / what they're worried about. But the whole gamut has the dynamic I mentioned before: pattern matching to AI content is adaptive to avoid sloppy thinking. It's unclear, from the reader's perspective, how much was used. So when alarm bells go off, it's usually because something feels off. Less about the individual piece, but about the distribution of content that it could be coming from. 

I hear you saying "come on, I put so much thought/effort, it's lazy to dismiss writing that feels like AI just because it has certain aesthetic modes". Potentially true, especially in *your* case. Though the adversarial attentional dynamics still exist.

(FWIW, I'm not personally too concerned with "AI was used", and more "AI enables sloppy thinking to look like good thinking, and this is epistemically adaptive to avoid")

text:
Yeah, admittedly, it's up there on the pareto curve. There are far cheaper ways to get lumens into eyeballs.

I like the form factor. It feels nice, is sturdy, looks good. And the CRI/no flicker/tunable color temp is hard to do cheap. FWIW, I do change the color temp often, and notice lower CRI quite a bit.

raw.full_text:
Yeah, admittedly, it's up there on the pareto curve. There are far cheaper ways to get lumens into eyeballs.

I like the form factor. It feels nice, is sturdy, looks good. And the CRI/no flicker/tunable color temp is hard to do cheap. FWIW, I do change the color temp often, and notice lower CRI quite a bit.

text:
And we're deep into the phenomenological, so I'm unsure how much is "I've convinced myself" / "this seems right for me" vs how much generalizes.

Low CRI is noticeable to me out in the world. I can kind of trace a biological argument that the light should be fuller spectrum as well, but low confidence (and if I really believed it, I'd use those IR chicken lamps probably).

Color temp is important for me because I'm more confident there's a circadian cycle connection (I want blue in the morning), *and* it'd make the lamp useless in the evening if I couldn't tune it down.

text:
I must elaborate. Christopher Alexander is a brilliant student of things as they appear, carefully noticing the field of care for properties and structure.

Don't read for the metaphysics / philosophy, fwiw. It's substance-monism, people have wrestled with subjective vs objective for a long time, etc. It's messy and I don't think accomplishes much for you.

Read for the phenomenology. In the author's note:

"In the same way, I have attempted to put together a workable explanation of the physical and emotional phenomena which I have observed in buildings and in the living world... Not only do we want to understand this set of phenomena, but we must also be able to reproduce them: we want to be able to create living structure in the world. Quantum mechanics would not have been of more than academic interest to a few university professors if it were not for its immense field of practical applications, such as in electronics. Here, too, in the sphere of building, we have a practical aim. We wish to create living structure in the built world; we wish to apply this model of the universe in order to reproduce the phenomena that we are interested in."

This isn't quite Popper's falsifiable-predictions claim. A theory is justified because it's workable. You can reproduce observable phenomena and build/do things!

It's useful to assume he's directionally right about the phenomenology of centers, the "I", beauty, wholeness, etc. He cared more about this than anyone I've encountered.

So there's a lot to learn from him. His workable explanation, imho, is best read as "this is how things appear" *and* "if taken as a model, you can uncover this quality that is meaningful".

The Luminous Ground is when he gently steps away from trying to work within a system of architecture to understand the generative function. And it's beautiful!

text:
A constructive account of Alexander:

There is a natural tension between coherence (separation and wholeness), operating at every scale. His fifteen properties are its grammar.

Levels of scale, strong centers, boundaries, alternating repetition, positive space, good shape, local symmetries, deep interlock, contrast, gradients, roughness, echoes, the void, inner calm, not-separateness.

Take any seriously, and you can notice more about how coherence is showing up.

Integrated-differentiation and criticality are substrate-independent, and common across nature, because all are products of adaptive growth under constraint. As such, we're keenly tuned towards it.

And our field of care isn't limited to bounded systems. Everything we touch is dependently-originated, intimately tied together. This doesn't need any sort of extra cosmic substrate or independent field.

The book is a love letter to Alexander's (and, our) field of care.

text:
(I'm an uninterested party, I've read simplytheseen, but practice in an entirely different system. Though I think I understand some of the discord)

The system being taught can be quite helpful, valuable, etc to people, but is using precise, technical terminology (not by accident).

Michael's vibe seems to be "yes, we aren't teaching theravada, this isn't being claimed, it's fine".

The term-overload may be confusing at the least (the terms mean *very* different things), and misleading at worst. This is the "he’s teaching is not the Buddhist fetters... it’s completely unambiguous". As an uninterested party, agreed — the collection of technical terms are unambiguously talking about different things, standards, contexts, etc.

I know at least a few people who didn't realize this, and thought they understood what was being accomplished in a traditional context... the goals / meta-goals, outcomes, phenomenology, etc.

It seems no harm is meant, the system is quite effective for people, etc. So that's not really the issue. This distinction may not matter to you or others.

Rather than, for example, being more effective in the world, understanding patterns of (beautiful) desire, etc.

When I think of some of the most important / meaningful times of my life, reducing suffering doesn't feel like an aspect that mattered so much?
